proposals.title: MetaCost: Fast Adaptation of Pre-trained Inference Cost Models to Novel Hardware via Meta-Learning
proposals.motivation: Efficient deployment of large language models (LLMs) requires accurate cost models for batching and scheduling, as seen in BlendServe. However, training a new cost model from scratch for each novel hardware platform (e.g., new accelerators, cloud instances) is prohibitively expensive in terms of data collection and compute. This creates a significant barrier to rapid, cost-optimal inference across diverse and evolving hardware environments.
proposals.hypothesis: A pre-trained cost model, meta-learned on a diverse set of hardware profiles, can be rapidly adapted to a novel, unseen hardware target with minimal fine-tuning samples, achieving accuracy comparable to a model trained from scratch on extensive target data.
proposals.method: We will pre-train a neural cost model (e.g., a Graph Neural Network over computational graphs) on a broad dataset of hardware performance profiles from the vLLM or DeepSpeed benchmark suites. We will then apply a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) framework, where each 'task' corresponds to a specific hardware configuration. The meta-learning objective is to find an initial model parameterization that can be adapted to a new hardware task via a few gradient steps using a small set of profiling data from the target. We will validate adaptation speed and prediction accuracy against baselines on held-out hardware.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical method for 'one-shot' or 'few-shot' cost model calibration, drastically reducing the profiling overhead for new hardware. It will contribute a publicly available meta-trained cost model and a benchmark for cross-hardware cost prediction, directly enhancing resource-aware inference systems.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been applied to NLP tasks, its application to the low-level system problem of hardware-specific inference cost modeling is novel. This approach moves beyond static, hardware-specific models (the current paradigm) towards a generalizable, adaptive cost predictor.

proposals.title: PipeSched: A Resource-Aware Scheduler for End-to-End Multi-Model LLM Pipelines
proposals.motivation: Modern LLM applications increasingly rely on pipelines of multiple models (e.g., retrieval, reasoning, critique, generation). Current serving systems like BlendServe optimize batching for single models, but treat pipelines as a series of independent stages, leading to suboptimal resource utilization, high latency, and wasted memory due to inter-stage idling and redundant caching. A holistic scheduler is needed to optimize the entire pipeline as a single, schedulable unit.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a scheduler with a global view of an entire multi-model pipeline can dynamically co-optimize batch composition, KV cache management, and stage placement across heterogeneous hardware to significantly improve aggregate throughput and reduce end-to-end latency compared to per-stage optimization.
proposals.method: We will extend the resource-aware batching paradigm of BlendServe to the pipeline level. The core is a PipeSched scheduler that models the pipeline as a directed graph of model stages. It will use a cost model incorporating per-stage latency, memory footprint (including KV cache), and inter-stage data movement. A dynamic programming algorithm will determine optimal batch sizing and routing across stages, while a shared, managed KV cache pool will be implemented to reuse intermediate activations across related requests within a pipeline execution window, reducing memory pressure and recomputation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) the first open-source scheduler specifically designed for end-to-end LLM pipelines, 2) a novel method for shared KV cache management across pipeline stages, and 3) a comprehensive benchmark demonstrating performance gains on realistic multi-model workloads (e.g., RAG chains, LLM-as-judge).
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes single-model inference, PipeSched introduces the novel abstraction of a 'pipeline batch' and a scheduling algorithm that makes joint decisions across dependent models, moving beyond the isolated stage optimization of current systems.

proposals.title: SoloServe: A Resource-Aware Scheduler for High-Throughput, Low-Latency LLM Serving in Sparse Request Environments
proposals.motivation: Current LLM serving systems, like those optimized in BlendServe, prioritize batch efficiency, which can incur high latency and resource waste under low or sporadic request loads common in interactive applications. As LLMs are deployed more widely in user-facing, real-time settings (e.g., chatbots, coding assistants), optimizing for many distinct, single-request concurrent queries is critical for cost-effective and responsive service.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a scheduler explicitly prioritizing single-request efficiency—by minimizing memory footprint per request and enabling fine-grained, dynamic resource sharing—can achieve higher aggregate throughput and lower tail latency for concurrent, non-batchable requests compared to batch-optimized systems, without sacrificing per-request computational efficiency.
proposals.method: We will design a novel serving scheduler that treats each request as an independent, schedulable unit with a dedicated lightweight context cache. The core method involves: 1) A resource-aware dispatcher that profiles GPU memory and compute utilization to pack non-uniform requests concurrently, 2) A modified continuous batching algorithm that groups requests only for fleeting, opportunistic kernel fusion when their execution paths align, otherwise executing them in true parallelism, and 3) A shared, tiered KV cache management system (inspired by vLLM's PagedAttention) but optimized for many small caches to reduce fragmentation and overhead.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new serving system architecture that redefines efficiency for sparse query loads, a set of benchmarks for evaluating single-request concurrency, and empirical evidence challenging the batch-first design paradigm. It will directly enable more scalable and affordable deployment of LLMs in interactive, variable-load scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While existing systems (e.g., Orca, vLLM) improve batch throughput, and BlendServe optimizes offline batching, SoloServe uniquely shifts the optimization target to the many-small-requests regime, co-designing the scheduler and memory manager for this neglected but critical workload.

proposals.title: FlexGen-Live: A Resource-Aware Hybrid Batching System for Low-Latency, High-Throughput LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: The widespread deployment of LLMs in interactive applications like chatbots and copilots demands low per-token latency for user responsiveness. However, existing high-throughput systems like FlexGen and BlendServe prioritize aggregate batch efficiency, often at the cost of increased latency for individual requests. This creates a critical gap in serving infrastructure that must simultaneously handle fluctuating, real-time traffic while maintaining strict latency Service Level Objectives (SLOs).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid batching system, which dynamically partitions computational and memory resources between a low-latency, continuous batching pathway and a high-throughput, speculative execution pathway, can significantly reduce tail latency for interactive requests without sacrificing overall system throughput.
proposals.method: We will extend the FlexGen framework with a two-tier scheduler. The first tier uses a real-time priority queue and continuous batching for interactive requests, employing KV cache management techniques from vLLM to minimize preemption overhead. The second tier processes batched, non-latency-sensitive requests using FlexGen's high-throughput methods, potentially augmented with speculative execution from draft models. A central resource-aware controller, informed by a lightweight profiler, will dynamically allocate GPU memory and compute slices (e.g., via CUDA streams) between the two tiers based on instantaneous load and SLO targets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified open-source system capable of mixed interactive and batch workloads, a common production scenario not well-addressed by prior art. We will contribute a novel scheduling algorithm and a comprehensive benchmark, extending Chatbot Arena's evaluation to include latency-throughput Pareto frontiers under realistic, variable load patterns.
proposals.novelty: While continuous batching and high-throughput offline inference are known, their co-design within a single, resource-constrained runtime with dynamic partitioning is novel. This moves beyond optimizing for a single workload type and directly addresses the tension between latency and throughput in multi-tenant serving environments.

proposals.title: FlexArch: Extending High-Throughput Inference via Offloading and Compression to Non-Autoregressive and Sparse Model Architectures
proposals.motivation: Current high-throughput inference systems like FlexGen are highly optimized for dense, autoregressive Transformer models (e.g., OPT, GPT), but the field is diversifying. Emerging architectures, such as non-autoregressive models for faster generation and Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models for scaling, present unique memory and compute patterns that break existing offloading heuristics. Efficiently serving these diverse models on resource-constrained hardware is a critical, unsolved problem for the broader deployment of advanced LLMs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by developing architecture-aware scheduling and compression strategies, the core principles of FlexGen can be generalized to support non-autoregressive and sparse MoE models, achieving comparable throughput and latency benefits without sacrificing model quality.
proposals.method: We will first profile the memory access patterns and computational graphs of target architectures (e.g., a non-autoregressive Transformer and an open-source MoE like SwitchTransformer). Based on this, we will extend FlexGen's offloading scheduler with two key components: 1) a dynamic expert-aware scheduler for MoEs that selectively loads active experts to GPU, and 2) a chunked pre-computation strategy for non-autoregressive models to optimize offloaded KV cache generation. We will integrate structured sparsity-aware compression (e.g., block-wise quantization) tailored to these patterns and evaluate throughput, latency, and memory usage on benchmarks derived from Chatbot Arena.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source inference engine capable of efficiently running a wider class of modern LLM architectures on limited hardware. It will provide the first systematic analysis of offloading trade-offs for non-autoregressive and MoE models, offering new benchmarking insights. This expands the practical applicability of high-throughput inference techniques beyond the standard Transformer paradigm.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes inference for a single architecture family, this proposal explicitly tackles the scheduling and compression challenges introduced by architectural deviations like conditional computation (MoEs) and parallel token generation, which require fundamental changes to offloading logic.

proposals.title: FlashFetch: A Predictive I/O and Data Movement Scheduler for Efficient LLM Offloading
proposals.motivation: The deployment of large language models (LLMs) via offloading (e.g., FlexGen, DeepSpeed-Zero-Infinity) is critical for resource-constrained environments, but its performance is bottlenecked by slow disk I/O and inefficient CPU-GPU data transfer. These overheads severely degrade inference throughput and latency, limiting the practical utility of high-capability models on commodity hardware. Optimizing this pipeline is essential for making advanced LLMs more accessible and cost-effective.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by co-designing a predictive, workload-aware I/O scheduler with a unified memory-mapped cache for model parameters, we can significantly reduce the stall time caused by disk access and data movement, thereby achieving near-in-memory inference throughput for offloaded LLMs.
proposals.method: We will develop FlashFetch, a system that integrates: 1) A lightweight profiler to analyze and predict the access patterns of different LLM layers during multi-round, variable-length inference sessions, building on the resource-aware batching concepts from BlendServe. 2) A kernel-level I/O scheduler that uses these patterns to perform asynchronous, batched prefetching of required model blocks from NVMe storage into a pinned host buffer. 3) A unified, memory-mapped cache (UMM-Cache) that serves as a staging area for both disk-originating and GPU-evicted parameters, minimizing redundant copies and enabling direct GPU DMA transfers via CUDA's user-space APIs.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a significant speedup in end-to-end token generation latency for offloaded LLM inference, validated through a new benchmark suite measuring I/O-bound performance. It will provide the community with an open-source, pluggable optimization layer compatible with existing offloading frameworks, advancing the state-of-the-art in resource-efficient inference.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes batching or caching in isolation, FlashFetch's novelty lies in the tight co-design of predictive I/O scheduling with a unified memory space that bypasses traditional copy overheads, directly targeting the combined disk-access and data-movement bottleneck identified in real-world, multi-turn LLM dialogues.

proposals.title: Cost-Based Optimization for Inference Serving: A Query Planner for Dynamic Tensor Computation and Data Movement
proposals.motivation: The efficient serving of large language models (LLMs) is bottlenecked by the static nature of current batching and scheduling strategies, which fail to adapt to fluctuating request patterns and heterogeneous hardware resources. This leads to suboptimal throughput and high latency, especially for complex, multi-step inference tasks. Drawing inspiration from decades of database research on adaptive query optimization could unlock significant performance gains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling an inference workload as a query plan over tensor operations and applying a cost-based optimizer—using a dynamic cost model of compute, memory bandwidth, and KV cache pressure—can generate execution strategies that significantly improve aggregate serving throughput and tail latency compared to static batching.
proposals.method: We will develop a prototype inference server that treats a batch of requests as a relational algebra expression over tensor operators. We will design a cost model that estimates the latency and memory overhead of candidate execution plans (e.g., different batching, fusion, and data movement strategies) based on real-time hardware telemetry and request characteristics. A dynamic planner, akin to a database query optimizer, will use this model to select the lowest-cost plan, integrating techniques from Zheng's prior work on resource-aware batching (BlendServe) and KV cache management.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, adaptive framework for LLM inference serving that systematically minimizes resource contention. We will release a new benchmark suite for evaluating dynamic inference schedulers under mixed workloads and demonstrate measurable improvements in throughput-latency trade-offs on platforms like Chatbot Arena.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes individual aspects like batching or caching, this is the first to formulate the end-to-end inference scheduling problem as a cost-based query optimization task, enabling holistic, dynamic planning of tensor computation and data movement across heterogeneous resources.

proposals.title: StreamServe: A Dataflow Architecture for High-Throughput, Low-Latency LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: Current LLM serving systems, including those using dynamic batching like BlendServe, treat the inference pipeline as a monolithic sequence of operations, leading to inefficient resource utilization and suboptimal latency-throughput trade-offs. As models and request volumes grow, a more granular, asynchronous execution model is needed to maximize hardware efficiency, especially for heterogeneous workloads with varying sequence lengths and priorities. This problem is critical for scaling real-world applications built on top of models evaluated in platforms like Chatbot Arena.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling the LLM inference pipeline (prefill, decode, KV cache management) as a fine-grained, asynchronous dataflow graph, we can achieve superior hardware utilization and lower latency than state-of-the-art batched schedulers, without compromising model accuracy or requiring model modifications.
proposals.method: We will design a streaming dataflow execution engine where core operations (e.g., attention blocks, MLP layers, token sampling) are nodes in a graph, connected by queues. We will implement a resource-aware scheduler that dynamically maps these nodes to GPU/CPU resources, enabling inter-request and intra-request parallelism (e.g., overlapping the decode step of one request with the prefill of another). Key algorithms will include a deadline-aware token dispatch policy and a graph partitioning strategy to minimize communication and maximize pipeline parallelism, building upon prior work in high-performance computing and real-time stream processing.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, open-source serving system architecture that decouples logical inference stages from physical execution, 2) a set of scheduling primitives for optimizing latency and throughput simultaneously under resource constraints, and 3) a comprehensive benchmark demonstrating performance gains over existing batching-based systems like vLLM and TGI on diverse workloads derived from Chatbot Arena logs.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes batching, this proposal fundamentally re-architects the inference executor itself using a dataflow paradigm, treating individual tokens and layers as streaming units. This moves beyond the sequential 'batch-of-sequences' execution model to enable unprecedented levels of asynchronous overlap and resource elasticity.

proposals.title: MicroLLM: Resource-Aware Batching and Extreme Memory Compression for Sub-100KB Language Model Inference on Microcontrollers
proposals.motivation: Deploying LLMs on microcontrollers (MCUs) with KBs of RAM is a critical frontier for ubiquitous, low-cost, and private AI, but current model compression and inference techniques are designed for systems with orders of magnitude more memory. Pre-2026 work on efficient inference, like BlendServe, focuses on server-scale batching, leaving a vast gap for extreme edge devices where memory is the primary constraint, not just throughput.
proposals.hypothesis: By co-designing a novel inference runtime with aggressive, multi-stage memory aggregation and a specialized, ultra-compact model architecture, it is possible to achieve useful, deterministic language tasks on general-purpose MCUs while staying within a 100KB RAM budget.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-pronged approach: (1) A 'Memory-Aware Micro-Batcher' that extends concepts from BlendServe to manage KB-scale KV caches and activation memory across multiple ultra-tiny models or tasks, treating system RAM as a unified resource pool. (2) An extreme compression pipeline combining 1-bit quantization, weight pruning, and attention distillation, targeting a novel 'MCU-Transformer' architecture with under 1M parameters. We will benchmark on ARM Cortex-M4/M7 platforms using adapted versions of Chatbot Arena's pairwise evaluation framework for objective quality assessment.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first open-source inference runtime and model family capable of running LLM-like tasks on standard MCUs, establishing a rigorous benchmark for sub-100KB AI. It will also provide new insights into the failure modes and vacillation behaviors (extending 'Ask Again, Then Fail') of models under extreme memory pressure, informing robust edge AI design.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior model compression work that targets smartphones or RPis, this proposal uniquely focuses on the co-design of runtime memory management and model architecture for the severe KB-memory regime of MCUs, applying and radically adapting data center-originated resource optimization principles to the microcontroller domain.

proposals.title: Aggregated Inference: Scaling LLM Serving to Heterogeneous Clusters of Low-Memory GPUs
proposals.motivation: The high cost of high-memory GPUs is a major barrier to accessible LLM inference. While prior work like BlendServe optimizes batching for single, powerful nodes, many organizations have clusters of older, lower-memory GPUs that are underutilized. This research addresses the critical problem of efficiently serving modern, large models across such heterogeneous, memory-constrained hardware, democratizing access to high-throughput inference.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by extending the aggregation paradigm—decoupling prompt processing, token generation, and result aggregation—to a multi-GPU, low-memory setting, we can achieve competitive aggregate throughput and latency compared to a single high-memory GPU, while significantly improving resource utilization and cost-efficiency.
proposals.method: We will design a distributed inference system that partitions a large model across multiple low-memory GPUs using tensor parallelism within a node and pipeline parallelism across nodes. The core innovation is a resource-aware scheduler that dynamically maps the 'prompt worker', 'generation worker', and 'aggregation' stages from the BlendServe architecture to the most suitable GPU based on available memory, compute, and interconnect bandwidth. We will develop a new cost model and orchestration layer to minimize communication overhead and manage KV cache placement across the distributed memory hierarchy.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel system architecture for cost-effective LLM inference on heterogeneous, low-memory GPU clusters, 2) a set of scheduling and caching algorithms optimized for disaggregated inference stages, and 3) an open-source implementation and benchmark demonstrating the trade-offs between cost, throughput, and latency on real hardware.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior aggregation work focused on CPU/disk offloading from a single GPU, this proposal fundamentally rethinks the aggregation approach for a fully distributed, multi-GPU environment, treating the entire cluster as a pooled, tiered memory system for LLM inference.

proposals.title: Serving Trillion-Parameter Models on Commodity Nodes: A Resource-Aware, Hierarchical Caching Framework
proposals.motivation: The scaling of large language models beyond 1 trillion parameters promises significant gains in capability, but inference on such models is currently restricted to massive, expensive GPU clusters. Enabling their deployment on a single commodity node would democratize access to frontier AI and unlock new applications. This directly addresses the core challenge of balancing model size, inference throughput, and memory constraints, a key theme in Zheng's prior work on resource-aware batching and system optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a novel, hierarchical caching mechanism, combined with extreme model compression and dynamic resource-aware scheduling, can enable the effective inference of trillion-parameter models on a single commodity GPU node without catastrophic performance degradation.
proposals.method: We will develop a system that integrates: 1) A multi-tier cache (GPU HBM, GPU VRAM, CPU RAM, NVMe SSD) for storing and swapping model parameters based on access patterns learned during inference, extending concepts from BlendServe. 2) Aggressive yet stable quantization methods (e.g., FP4/INT4) combined with selective activation of high-precision expert layers within a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture. 3) A dynamic scheduler that profiles request complexity (inspired by Chatbot Arena benchmarks) to optimize batching and cache prefetching, minimizing the latency impact of swapping.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a fully open-sourced, end-to-end framework for serving extreme-scale models on limited hardware, pushing the boundary of feasible model size per node. It will provide new benchmarks for inference efficiency under severe memory constraints and offer insights into the stability of highly quantized, trillion-parameter models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes batching or caching in isolation, our approach uniquely co-designs hierarchical caching, aggressive quantization, and MoE routing specifically for the trillion-parameter regime on a single node, a previously unexplored systems challenge. The integration of learned access patterns for parameter prefetching is a novel extension of offline inference optimization.

proposals.title: PagedBatch: Co-designing KV Cache Paging and Continuous Batching for High-Throughput, Low-Latency LLM Serving
proposals.motivation: The deployment of large language models (LLMs) for real-time services is bottlenecked by GPU memory constraints and inefficient request scheduling. While PagedAttention (vLLM) optimizes KV cache memory utilization and continuous batching (e.g., in Orca, TensorRT-LLM) improves GPU throughput, they operate as independent optimizations. A co-designed system is needed to unlock further performance gains by resolving their conflicting interactions, such as fragmentation from dynamic batching and non-contiguous paged memory access.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a tight integration of the PagedAttention memory manager with the continuous batching scheduler, through a resource-aware joint optimization policy, will significantly improve both latency and throughput over state-of-the-art systems like vLLM and BlendServe.
proposals.method: We will develop PagedBatch, a novel serving system that integrates a modified PagedAttention allocator with a continuous batching scheduler. The core innovation is a resource-aware batching algorithm that uses real-time KV cache page layout and fragmentation metrics as first-class scheduling signals. We will implement a cost model that predicts the latency impact of batching decisions on memory access patterns, enabling the scheduler to batch requests with compatible memory footprints. The system will be evaluated using benchmarks from Chatbot Arena and synthetic workloads on GPU clusters.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver 1) a new open-source LLM serving system with co-designed memory and scheduling managers, 2) a formal model and analysis of the memory-batching trade-off space, and 3) a comprehensive empirical evaluation demonstrating superior throughput-latency Pareto frontiers compared to existing systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work applies memory paging and dynamic batching separately, PagedBatch's novelty lies in its joint optimization loop, where the batching scheduler explicitly accounts for and minimizes the latency penalty induced by non-contiguous KV cache access patterns.

proposals.title: CacheFlow: Dynamic KV Cache Management for Efficient Multi-Query and Grouped-Query Attention Inference
proposals.motivation: Multi-query attention (MQA) and grouped-query attention (GQA) are widely adopted in models like Llama 2 and PaLM to reduce the memory footprint of the KV cache during inference, a major bottleneck for high-throughput serving. However, existing KV cache management systems, such as vLLM's PagedAttention, are designed for standard multi-head attention and do not exploit the unique sharing patterns of keys and values in MQA/GQA. This leads to suboptimal memory utilization and redundant computation, limiting throughput and scalability for these efficient attention variants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a KV cache management system explicitly designed for the parameter sharing semantics of MQA and GQA can significantly reduce memory overhead and improve inference throughput compared to treating them as standard multi-head attention, without compromising output correctness.
proposals.method: We will design and implement CacheFlow, a resource-aware KV cache manager integrated into a system like BlendServe. First, we will develop a new cache metadata structure that tracks the sharing relationships of KV blocks across attention heads in MQA/GQA, enabling a single physical block to serve multiple logical heads. Second, we will create a dynamic paging and eviction policy that prioritizes shared blocks to maximize memory savings. Third, we will build a scheduler that batches requests with compatible attention patterns to increase the effective sharing rate. We will evaluate using benchmarks from Chatbot Arena and synthetic workloads.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic analysis and optimization framework for KV cache management in MQA/GQA models. We will release an open-source, high-throughput inference system module that demonstrates substantial improvements in requests-per-second and memory efficiency for popular models like Llama 2, directly advancing the state of efficient LLM serving.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes KV cache management for standard attention, CacheFlow is novel in formally modeling and exploiting the constrained sharing patterns intrinsic to MQA and GQA architectures to redesign core cache allocation and scheduling algorithms.

proposals.title: CacheSqueeze: Transparent OS-Level Memory Compression for KV Cache in LLM Serving
proposals.motivation: The KV cache is a critical bottleneck for high-throughput LLM inference, consuming gigabytes of memory per concurrent request and limiting batch sizes. Existing solutions like PagedAttention require intrusive kernel modifications or application-level logic. A transparent, system-level approach is needed to increase effective memory capacity and improve hardware utilization without altering the model or serving framework.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying transparent memory compression techniques, inspired by OS (e.g., zswap) and graphics pipeline methods, to KV cache pages under memory pressure can significantly increase the effective batch size and throughput of LLM serving systems without degrading latency or requiring application changes.
proposals.method: We will develop a kernel module that intercepts and compresses KV cache pages managed by the vLLM's PagedAttention allocator. Using a lightweight compression algorithm (e.g., LZ4), we will implement a compressed cache pool in a reserved memory zone (ZONE_MOVABLE). We will design an adaptive policy, triggered by a global memory pressure metric, to selectively compress least-recently-used KV cache blocks. The system will be evaluated using the Chatbot Arena workload trace on a cluster with constrained memory, measuring throughput (requests/sec) and P99 latency against a vLLM baseline.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a production-ready, transparent system for memory-efficient LLM serving that boosts throughput under memory constraints. It will provide a detailed analysis of the trade-offs between compression overhead, latency, and batch size, offering a new, orthogonal tool for system optimizers beyond algorithmic KV cache eviction or quantization.
proposals.novelty: While memory compression is well-studied in OS contexts, its application to the specific access patterns and performance requirements of the KV cache is novel. This approach is distinct from prior LLM-specific work like BlendServe as it operates transparently below the application layer, requiring no changes to batching logic or model architecture.

proposals.title: FleetCache: Virtual Memory Paging for Dense, Heterogeneous Multi-Model Serving
proposals.motivation: The AI serving landscape is diversifying beyond monolithic LLMs to include many specialized, smaller models (e.g., for coding, moderation, translation). Serving this heterogeneous fleet efficiently on a single GPU cluster is challenging due to severe memory fragmentation and underutilization from statically partitioning memory per model. This limits total throughput and increases cost, hindering the practical deployment of composite AI applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying virtual memory paging principles—specifically demand paging, a unified address space, and a working-set-based replacement policy—to GPU memory management will enable higher density and throughput when serving a dynamic set of heterogeneous small models compared to static partitioning or naive LRU eviction.
proposals.method: We will design FleetCache, a serving system that treats the aggregate GPU memory as a unified virtual address space and each model's parameters as pages. An online profiler will characterize each model's 'working set'—the critical layers for a given throughput SLA. A lightweight scheduler, integrated with a system like BlendServe, will use this to make pre-fetch and eviction decisions, swapping model pages between GPU HBM and CPU RAM via NVLink/PCIe. We will benchmark using diverse model families (e.g., CodeLlama, Flan-T5, Whisper) under synthetic and real-world traces from platforms like Chatbot Arena.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new system design for dense multi-model serving, a benchmark for evaluating such systems, and quantitative evidence that paging-inspired memory management can significantly improve aggregate throughput and GPU utilization for heterogeneous workloads.
proposals.novelty: While paging is classic in OS, its application to managing *active* inference workloads of many heterogeneous models on GPUs is novel, moving beyond caching for a single model or homogeneous batching. It directly addresses the fragmentation problem ignored by prior model-serving systems.

proposals.title: CacheFusion: Dynamic Memory Management for Multi-Candidate KV Caches in Speculative Decoding
proposals.motivation: Speculative decoding significantly accelerates LLM inference by using a smaller draft model to propose multiple candidate token sequences, which are then verified in parallel by the larger target model. However, this creates a dual memory problem: the KV cache for each candidate path must be stored separately during verification, leading to prohibitive and unpredictable GPU memory overhead that limits batch sizes and throughput gains. Efficiently managing this explosion of intermediate states is a critical bottleneck for deploying speculative decoding in production systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dynamic, resource-aware memory manager can intelligently allocate, share, and evict KV cache blocks across speculative candidates, dramatically reducing peak memory usage without compromising verification accuracy, thereby unlocking higher and more consistent inference throughput.
proposals.method: We will design CacheFusion, a memory manager integrated into a system like vLLM or BlendServe. It will employ: 1) A candidate-aware block allocator that identifies and shares common KV cache prefixes across speculative branches. 2) A lightweight cost-benefit model to predict the utility of retaining partial candidate caches, enabling proactive eviction of low-probability branches. 3) A just-in-time regeneration mechanism using the draft model for evicted cache segments if a candidate is accepted, trading minimal compute for substantial memory savings. We will benchmark against static allocation baselines using Chatbot Arena models and workloads.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic framework for managing the memory footprint of multi-candidate speculative execution. We will deliver a production-ready system component that increases effective batch sizes under memory constraints, making speculative decoding more viable for latency-sensitive services. The research will also offer new insights into the trade-offs between memory, compute, and acceptance rates in speculative inference.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes batching (BlendServe) or PagedAttention for single sequences, CacheFusion uniquely addresses the inter-candidate memory dependencies and volatility inherent to speculative decoding. Its predictive eviction and selective regeneration policies are novel for KV cache management.

proposals.title: Human-in-the-Loop Dynamics: Modeling User Query Patterns and Latency Tolerance from Large-Scale LLM Interaction Logs
proposals.motivation: Current LLM inference optimization research, including work on resource-aware batching and caching, focuses primarily on model-centric metrics like throughput and latency. However, real-world system efficiency is co-determined by user behavior, which is poorly understood. Analyzing interaction datasets as records of human activity, rather than just LLM performance, is crucial for designing systems that align with actual user needs and patterns.
proposals.hypothesis: User query patterns (e.g., burstiness, session length, topic shifts) and subjective latency tolerance are predictable and can be modeled to create more efficient, human-aware inference scheduling and caching policies that outperform purely model-centric optimizations.
proposals.method: We will analyze large-scale, anonymized interaction logs from platforms like Chatbot Arena, treating sequences of user prompts and their associated timing as a behavioral dataset. We will develop statistical and lightweight machine learning models (e.g., Hawkes processes, LSTM networks) to predict query arrival bursts and session boundaries. Concurrently, we will use implicit feedback (e.g., follow-up query speed) and explicit ratings to model user-specific latency tolerance. These models will be integrated into a resource-aware scheduler (extending frameworks like BlendServe) to dynamically adjust batching and caching strategies based on predicted user state.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce the first formal framework for modeling user behavior in LLM serving systems. It will yield new, user-aware algorithms for inference scheduling that improve perceived responsiveness and system efficiency. The resulting public dataset of annotated user interaction patterns will provide a valuable resource for human-computer interaction research in conversational AI.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses interaction data to rank LLMs (Chatbot Arena) or diagnose model inconsistencies (Ask Again, Then Fail), this proposal inverts the lens to model the human user explicitly for systems research. It moves beyond treating queries as independent, identically distributed tasks, instead optimizing for the observed temporal and behavioral structure of human conversations.

proposals.title: LiveJudge: A High-Throughput, Low-Latency System for Continuous LLM Evaluation in Production
proposals.motivation: Current LLM evaluation relies on static benchmarks and costly, infrequent human assessments, failing to capture real-world performance drift and user interaction patterns. As models are deployed at scale, there is a critical need for continuous, automated evaluation to ensure quality, safety, and alignment. This gap limits our ability to rapidly iterate on and reliably monitor production LLMs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a system combining a high-throughput, resource-aware inference engine with a specialized LLM-as-a-Judge (LLM-J) pipeline can enable accurate, real-time, and cost-effective continuous evaluation of production LLM outputs at scale.
proposals.method: We will build LiveJudge by extending the resource-aware dynamic batching from BlendServe to optimize the LLM-J's inference throughput and latency. The system will employ a tiered caching strategy, storing frequent query-judge pairs to reduce compute costs, and a human feedback loop modeled on Chatbot Arena to periodically calibrate the judge. We will design a novel streaming benchmark to simulate live traffic and measure system performance under load.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source framework for live LLM evaluation, a new benchmark for streaming model assessment, and empirical findings on the stability and cost-effectiveness of LLM judges in production settings. It will provide a crucial tool for maintaining and improving deployed AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses LLMs as judges in offline settings, LiveJudge uniquely focuses on the systems challenges of integrating a judge into a live serving stack, optimizing for throughput and cost, and studying longitudinal judgment consistency on real-world data streams.

proposals.title: TransferCost: A Meta-Learning Framework for Generalizable Latency and Memory Cost Models in LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: Efficient inference for large language models (LLMs) relies on accurate cost models for operators like attention and feed-forward layers to optimize batching and scheduling, as seen in systems like BlendServe. However, training a precise cost model for each new model architecture or hardware configuration is prohibitively expensive. A method to transfer learned cost models across workloads would dramatically reduce the resource overhead of deploying optimized inference systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning framework can learn a shared representation of neural network operator characteristics (e.g., tensor shapes, data dependencies, memory access patterns) that enables a single cost model to generalize accurately across diverse model architectures and inference workloads.
proposals.method: We will develop a graph neural network (GNN) that encodes the computational graph of an operator or sub-network into a latent embedding. This embedding will be used as a conditioning input for a neural cost predictor (a small MLP) trained via model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML). The system will be trained on a diverse benchmark of profiled operator latencies and memory footprints from models like Llama, GPT, and Mixtral across GPUs with varying memory hierarchies. Inference-time adaptation will require only a few profiling samples from a new target workload.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, data-efficient framework for bootstrapping cost models for new LLMs and hardware, directly lowering the barrier to high-throughput, resource-aware inference. We will contribute a new benchmark for cost model transferability and open-source the trained meta-model, enabling more adaptive systems akin to a learned version of the caching and batching optimizations in BlendServe.
proposals.novelty: While prior work profiles costs for specific models, we propose a *learned, transferable* cost model that abstracts operator semantics, moving beyond simple regression on raw parameters. This approach is novel compared to static analytical models or workload-specific profiling.

proposals.title: DynaShape: A Library for Dynamic Shape-Aware Just-in-Time Compilation in PyTorch and JAX
proposals.motivation: Modern ML workloads increasingly involve dynamic computational graphs, such as variable-length sequences in transformers, data-dependent control flow, and dynamic neural architectures. Existing compilation frameworks (e.g., TorchDynamo, JAX's jit) and kernel libraries (e.g., FlashAttention) are optimized for static shapes, leading to suboptimal performance or graph breaks when dimensions are unknown at compile time. This creates a fundamental tension between model flexibility and inference efficiency, a core challenge in Lianmin Zheng's prior work on resource-aware batching and throughput optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a library performing just-in-time compilation with explicit symbolic shape reasoning and runtime shape distribution profiling can generate and cache multiple optimized kernel variants, enabling near-optimal hardware performance for dynamic graphs without requiring developers to abandon their native framework APIs.
proposals.method: We will develop a library that integrates as a backend for PyTorch's TorchDynamo and JAX's jit. It extends the Halide/AutoTVM auto-scheduling cost model to incorporate symbolic shape constraints and shape-invariant pattern recognition. During execution, the system will profile runtime shape distributions to identify a discrete set of frequent shape configurations. For each, it will generate and cache an optimized kernel variant using template-based code generation. A lightweight runtime dispatcher, informed by a learned cost model, will select the optimal cached kernel. This approach will be evaluated on dynamic workloads like mixture-of-experts models and transformers with speculative decoding.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a production-ready open-source library that brings shape-specialized compilation to mainstream ML frameworks, directly improving inference throughput and memory efficiency for dynamic models. It will provide a systematic methodology for profiling and optimizing shape-variable computation graphs, advancing the state of the art in resource-aware inference systems.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior JIT compilers that treat dynamic shapes as a barrier to optimization, DynaShape explicitly models shape distributions to drive multi-variant kernel generation and caching. This integrates symbolic reasoning and profile-guided optimization into a unified, framework-agnostic library, a step beyond static compilation or manual kernel fusion.

proposals.title: HardwareGen: Co-Designing Specialized Accelerators via Learned LLM Inference Cost Models
proposals.motivation: The explosive growth of large language models (LLMs) has created a critical mismatch between general-purpose hardware (e.g., GPUs) and the specific computational patterns of auto-regressive inference, leading to suboptimal throughput, high latency, and inefficient resource utilization. Current hardware design remains decoupled from the detailed, workload-specific cost models that recent systems research (like BlendServe) has begun to characterize. This proposal addresses the need for a closed-loop, data-driven methodology to generate hardware architectures tailored for emerging LLM inference workloads.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that learned, fine-grained cost models of LLM inference—capturing memory access patterns, kernel fusion opportunities, and batching dynamics—can be inverted to generate novel, efficient hardware architecture specifications that outperform general-purpose designs for a target workload distribution.
proposals.method: We will first extend the resource-aware cost models from BlendServe to create a differentiable proxy model of inference performance across a space of hardware parameters (e.g., memory hierarchy, compute unit ratios, on-chip SRAM size). Using this model as a reward function, we will employ a search algorithm (e.g., evolutionary search or reinforcement learning) to explore the architectural design space. The search will be conditioned on a benchmark distribution of real-world LLM inference traces (from sources like Chatbot Arena) and constrained by realistic physical limits (area, power). The output will be a set of Pareto-optimal hardware specifications (e.g., a modified TPU-v5 or GPU streaming multiprocessor blueprint).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel methodology for program/hardware co-design using learned cost models, 2) a set of concrete, generated hardware architecture specifications optimized for modern LLM inference, and 3) an open-source framework that allows systems researchers to explore hardware designs for their specific workload benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses cost models to schedule computations on fixed hardware, we invert this relationship to *generate* the hardware itself. This moves beyond traditional hardware/software co-design by leveraging modern, data-driven LLM performance models as the primary design driver.

proposals.title: Dual: Learning to Prune Inefficient Program Variants for High-Throughput LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: Optimizing inference for large language models (LLMs) is critical for cost and latency, but the search space of potential kernel implementations and batching strategies is vast. Current methods, like heuristic search or exhaustive profiling, are either slow or miss optimal configurations, directly impacting throughput and resource utilization. This creates a bottleneck for deploying efficient, scalable LLM services.
proposals.hypothesis: A lightweight, learned model can accurately and rapidly predict the relative performance (e.g., latency, memory usage) of candidate program variants, enabling effective pruning of a combinatorial search space to accelerate the discovery of near-optimal implementations.
proposals.method: We will train a dual-purpose transformer-based model on a dataset of program variants (e.g., from kernel fusion, attention optimizations, or BlendServe-style batching strategies) paired with profiled performance metrics. The model will ingest a structured representation of the variant (e.g., a computational graph with operator and resource annotations) and output a scalar score predicting its inefficiency relative to a baseline. This predictor will be integrated into a search algorithm to prune suboptimal branches early, focusing evaluation resources on promising candidates.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, data-driven tool for system optimization, significantly reducing the time needed to tune LLM inference configurations. It will advance the field of machine learning for systems by demonstrating effective learning-to-prune for a combinatorial optimization problem. The resulting framework will be validated on real-world inference benchmarks, showing measurable improvements in search efficiency and final throughput.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses learning for direct performance prediction or heuristic guidance, our approach specifically targets the pruning decision within a search process, framing it as a binary classification task to identify suboptimal variants. This integrates core themes from Zheng's prior work—throughput optimization, resource-aware batching, and benchmarking—into a unified, learned pruning agent.

proposals.title: LiveOpt: Online Re-optimization of LLM Inference via Continuous Runtime Profiling
proposals.motivation: Current LLM serving systems, like those in BlendServe, treat optimization as a static, offline compilation step based on anticipated workloads. In production, request patterns, input characteristics, and system load are dynamic and unpredictable, leading to suboptimal throughput and latency. A system that cannot adapt online wastes computational resources and degrades user experience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a continuous, online re-optimization framework for LLM inference, driven by lightweight runtime profiling, can dynamically adapt batching, scheduling, and memory management strategies to outperform static, offline-optimized systems in both throughput and tail latency.
proposals.method: We will extend a system like vLLM or BlendServe with a lightweight monitoring daemon that continuously profiles key metrics (e.g., request arrival distribution, KV cache hit rates, compute utilization). This profile will feed a reinforcement learning agent (e.g., a contextual bandit) that controls dynamic batching windows, eviction policies for the PagedAttention cache, and scheduling priorities. The agent's policy will be updated online using the profiled data, enabling the system to adapt to shifting workload phases without a full recompilation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel system architecture for online-adaptive LLM serving, 2) a set of lightweight profiling metrics and control knobs for runtime optimization, and 3) a benchmark demonstrating performance gains over static systems under variable, real-world trace workloads.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes offline, LiveOpt introduces continuous, closed-loop optimization into the inference serving stack, treating the runtime environment as a non-stationary process to be actively learned and adapted to, moving beyond one-pass compilation.

proposals.title: HeteroCost: A Learned Performance Model for LLM Inference Across Diverse Hardware Accelerators
proposals.motivation: Current learning-based cost models for LLM inference, such as those used in resource-aware batching (e.g., BlendServe), are primarily validated on mainstream CPUs and GPUs. The rapid emergence of specialized inference accelerators (e.g., Google TPUs, AWS Trainium/Inferentia, Groq LPUs, and novel ASICs) creates a fragmented landscape where efficient model serving and scheduling require accurate, portable performance predictors. A hardware-agnostic cost model is critical for optimizing throughput and resource utilization in heterogeneous data centers and edge deployments.
proposals.hypothesis: A single, learned cost modeling framework can be extended to accurately predict the latency and throughput of autoregressive LLM inference across a diverse set of hardware accelerators by incorporating a unified set of architecture-agnostic and hardware-specific microbenchmark features.
proposals.method: We will extend the core learning-based methodology from systems like BlendServe. First, we will design a comprehensive feature set encompassing model parameters (size, architecture), request characteristics (sequence lengths), and key hardware primitives (e.g., memory bandwidth, compute peak FLOPs, on-chip memory size) obtained via targeted microbenchmarks. We will then collect a large-scale performance dataset by executing a curated benchmark of LLM kernels (attention, FFN layers, KV cache operations) on target hardware including TPU v4/v5, AWS Inferentia2, and Groq LPUs. A hierarchical neural network model will be trained on this multi-hardware dataset to predict operation costs, which will be integrated into a scheduler simulator for evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first open, learned cost model for LLMs that generalizes across major classes of inference accelerators. It will enable more intelligent workload placement and batching in heterogeneous clusters, directly improving aggregate throughput and cost-efficiency. We will release the model, dataset, and benchmarking suite to advance research in cross-platform LLM systems optimization.
proposals.novelty: While learned cost models exist for GPUs/CPUs, applying them to the distinct execution models and architectures of TPUs, dataflow processors (Groq), and neural processors (Inferentia) is unexplored. The key novelty lies in identifying a minimal set of portable microbenchmarks that capture essential performance characteristics across these diverse paradigms.

proposals.title: UnifiedIR: Extending TVM for Agile Integration of Emerging Framework Operators and Optimized LLM Serving
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of specialized deep learning frameworks (e.g., JAX, OneFlow) and custom operator sets for novel model architectures, particularly large language models (LLMs), creates fragmentation. This hinders the deployment of state-of-the-art models on diverse hardware using unified compilation stacks like Apache TVM. Efficient LLM serving, a core research theme for Zheng, requires low-latency, high-throughput inference which is bottlenecked by the inability to natively import and fuse novel operators from research frameworks into TVM's optimization pipeline.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that developing a modular, declarative front-end extension mechanism for TVM, focused on a common high-level operator abstraction, will significantly reduce the integration overhead for new frameworks and operators, enabling more aggressive graph-level optimizations and ultimately improving inference latency and throughput for complex LLMs.
proposals.method: We will design a 'UnifiedIR' declarative schema that extends TVM's Relay IR to describe operator semantics and data layouts independently of source frameworks. We will implement importers for JAX and the emerging MLX framework, targeting their unique control flow and operator sets. A key component is a 'pattern matcher and replacer' that maps groups of imported framework-specific operators to fused, hardware-optimized kernels in TVM, leveraging prior work on resource-aware batching (BlendServe) and KV cache management. We will benchmark using LLM workloads from Chatbot Arena.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a scalable methodology for rapidly adding front-end support to TVM, 2) open-source importers for JAX and MLX with support for their autodiff and control-flow operators, and 3) a quantitative analysis showing reduced inference latency and improved system throughput for LLMs when using the unified import and fusion path compared to framework-specific runtime fallbacks.
proposals.novelty: While TVM supports multiple front-ends, our approach is novel in its focus on a declarative, semantics-first abstraction to decouple framework evolution from compiler support, and its direct integration with LLM-serving optimizations like selective batching and memory planning, creating a cohesive pipeline from novel model code to efficient deployment.

proposals.title: AutoScale-TVM: Automated Model Partitioning and Optimization for Distributed Heterogeneous Inference
proposals.motivation: The deployment of large language models (LLMs) is constrained by the memory and compute limits of single devices, leading to high latency and low throughput. Existing multi-device solutions require extensive manual effort for model partitioning and optimization, which is unsustainable for the rapidly evolving landscape of heterogeneous hardware (e.g., GPUs, TPUs, CPUs). An automated, compiler-based approach is needed to efficiently scale inference across diverse device clusters, directly addressing the resource and throughput challenges central to modern LLM serving.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by extending the TVM compiler stack with a cost-based, hardware-aware partitioning algorithm and a unified runtime for heterogeneous execution, we can automatically generate distributed execution plans that significantly improve inference throughput and reduce latency compared to manual or single-device deployments.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage compiler pass within TVM. First, a profiling-based cost model will analyze computational graphs, incorporating device-specific memory bandwidth, compute capability, and inter-device communication costs (inspired by resource-aware batching in BlendServe). Second, a dynamic programming algorithm will search for an optimal partition strategy, minimizing a combined cost function of latency and memory usage. The optimized plan will be compiled into a unified runtime that manages data movement, kernel execution, and synchronization across devices, leveraging techniques like speculative execution and cross-device operator fusion.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) an open-source extension to TVM for automated multi-device partitioning, 2) a novel cost model and optimization algorithm for heterogeneous clusters, and 3) a comprehensive benchmark suite evaluating the system's performance on large models (e.g., Llama 2, GPT-3 variants) across real-world hardware configurations, providing empirical data on scaling efficiency.
proposals.novelty: While TVM and related compilers optimize for single devices, AutoScale-TVM automates the end-to-end pipeline for *heterogeneous* multi-device inference, integrating hardware-aware cost modeling with a unified runtime—a significant step beyond manual pipeline parallelism or homogeneous data parallelism frameworks.

proposals.title: H2O-GPU: Hardware-Aware Hierarchical Cache Optimization for Efficient LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: The H2O (Heavy-Hitter Oracle) eviction algorithm significantly improves LLM inference throughput by managing KV cache memory. However, its design is largely hardware-agnostic, ignoring the performance-critical nuances of modern GPU memory hierarchies (e.g., HBM vs. SRAM bandwidth, access latencies). As models and context lengths grow, suboptimal cache placement and eviction decisions on specific hardware lead to underutilized memory bandwidth and increased latency, limiting the practical gains from such algorithms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tailoring the H2O eviction policy and cache data structure to explicitly model and exploit the bandwidth and latency characteristics of a target GPU's memory hierarchy will yield significant, measurable improvements in inference throughput and latency compared to the baseline hardware-agnostic H2O implementation.
proposals.method: We will develop a profiling suite to characterize key memory hierarchy parameters (e.g., HBM/SRAM bandwidth, cacheline sizes, access patterns) for target GPUs (e.g., NVIDIA H100, A100). Using this model, we will design a hierarchical cache structure that intelligently partitions and places 'heavy-hitter' KV pairs across faster memory tiers. The core eviction algorithm will be extended with a cost function that incorporates hardware-specific access penalties, making eviction decisions that minimize the expected latency cost per generated token. We will integrate and evaluate this system within the vLLM or FlexServe inference framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled methodology for co-designing KV cache management algorithms with underlying hardware, moving beyond one-size-fits-all solutions. We will deliver an open-source, optimized implementation (H2O-GPU) that sets a new state-of-the-art for hardware-aware LLM inference throughput, validated through rigorous benchmarks on models like Llama-2 and Mistral across diverse workloads.
proposals.novelty: While prior work like vLLM's PagedAttention and the original H2O optimize cache logic, they treat GPU memory as a uniform pool. Our approach is novel in explicitly modeling the GPU memory hierarchy to drive cache management decisions, a layer of optimization previously unexplored for KV cache eviction algorithms.

proposals.title: CacheGenius: Learning to Manage KV Cache for Efficient LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: The KV cache is critical for transformer inference latency and throughput, but its size grows linearly with sequence length, straining memory resources. Heuristic eviction policies (e.g., LRU) used in systems like vLLM are suboptimal as they ignore the semantic importance of cached tokens for future generation. An intelligent, predictive cache management strategy is needed to maximize cache utility under constrained memory, directly improving the cost and speed of serving LLMs.
proposals.hypothesis: A small, lightweight neural model can learn to predict the future utility of KV cache entries more accurately than static heuristics, enabling eviction decisions that improve overall cache hit rates and reduce token generation latency for a given memory budget.
proposals.method: We will train a lightweight MLP or micro-transformer on traces from LLM inference (e.g., LLaMA-2, Vicuna) to predict a token's likelihood of being re-accessed. Features will include positional, attention, and syntactic metadata. This learned index will be integrated into a modified PagedAttention system, where it scores cache entries for eviction. We will benchmark against LRU and random baselines using established inference workloads (e.g., ShareGPT, Chatbot Arena prompts) and measure wall-clock latency and throughput.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, learned component for production inference systems that directly optimizes a key memory bottleneck. It will yield a publicly available benchmark suite for cache policy evaluation and demonstrate measurable latency reductions for long-context dialogues, advancing the state-of-the-art in resource-efficient LLM serving.
proposals.novelty: While learned indices have been explored for database systems, their application to the specific problem of KV cache eviction in autoregressive LLM inference is novel. This approach moves beyond simple access recency to model the semantic and structural factors influencing token reuse.

proposals.title: ARC-KV: Adaptive Replacement Caching for Efficient Key-Value Cache Management in Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: The memory footprint of the Key-Value (KV) cache is a primary bottleneck for the throughput and latency of large language model (LLM) inference, especially for long-context and multi-turn serving. Existing systems often use simple eviction policies like FIFO or basic LRU, which are agnostic to the complex, non-uniform access patterns of attention heads across layers and tokens. This inefficiency limits the practical deployment of LLMs under constrained GPU memory.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sophisticated, self-tuning cache replacement policies from CPU architecture, such as Adaptive Replacement Cache (ARC) and LRU-K, can be adapted to the KV cache context to significantly improve cache hit rates, thereby increasing inference throughput and reducing memory pressure without degrading output quality.
proposals.method: We will design a lightweight profiling module to track access frequencies and recencies for KV cache blocks at a granularity of attention heads or token positions. We will then adapt the ARC algorithm, which dynamically balances between recent and frequent access histories, to operate within the transformer's autoregressive decoding loop. This involves modifying the inference scheduler to integrate the replacement decision logic and evaluating policies like LRU-2 and ARC against diverse benchmarks, including multi-document QA and long conversational tasks from Chatbot Arena.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, plug-and-play cache management layer for systems like vLLM or BlendServe, demonstrably improving effective context window utilization and batch throughput. It will provide the first systematic analysis of classical replacement policies in the KV cache domain, establishing new best practices for memory-efficient inference.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes KV cache via quantization or sparsity, directly porting and rigorously evaluating self-tuning CPU cache algorithms for the unique sequential, layered data access patterns of transformer inference is a novel and unexplored direction.

proposals.title: H2O at Scale: Enabling Efficient Million-Token Inference and Streaming for Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: As LLMs are applied to long-context tasks like code repositories, scientific literature, and multi-document analysis, processing million-token sequences becomes essential. However, the KV cache memory overhead of standard attention is prohibitive at this scale, severely limiting throughput and practical deployment. Efficient attention mechanisms like H2O (Heavy-Hitter Oracle) have shown promise for shorter contexts, but their behavior and optimization potential at extreme lengths remain unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the H2O algorithm, when combined with resource-aware batching and optimized cache management, can maintain high inference throughput and quality for contexts exceeding one million tokens, enabling practical long-context streaming generation.
proposals.method: We will extend the H2O algorithm, which dynamically evicts non-essential KV pairs based on attention scores, for extreme-length sequences. Building on BlendServe's resource-aware batching, we will design a hybrid caching strategy that combines H2O's eviction policy with tiered storage (GPU HBM and CPU RAM) for the massive KV cache. We will rigorously benchmark inference latency, memory footprint, and output quality (using human evaluation frameworks from Chatbot Arena) on synthetic and real-world long-document tasks, comparing against baseline methods like streaming LLM and windowed attention.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic evaluation and optimization of the H2O algorithm for million-token contexts, delivering a production-ready inference system for extreme long-context LLMs. It will establish new benchmarks for throughput and memory efficiency in long-context generation, directly advancing the field of resource-efficient inference.
proposals.novelty: While H2O has been studied for moderate contexts, its scaling laws, failure modes, and optimal integration with streaming generation for million-token sequences are unknown. The novelty lies in pushing this specific, promising algorithm to its practical limits, a critical step not yet taken in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: H2O for the Edge: Quantifying and Exploiting Heavy Hitters in Tiny Language Models for Efficient Inference
proposals.motivation: The deployment of small language models (SLMs) on edge devices is critical for privacy, latency, and cost, but is severely limited by memory and compute constraints. Pre-2026 research like H2O (Heavy-Hitter Oracle) has shown that caching the KV pairs of frequently accessed 'heavy hitter' tokens can drastically reduce inference cost for large models, but its existence and utility in sub-1B parameter models is unknown. This work investigates whether this phenomenon scales down, which is essential for enabling performant on-device AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that heavy hitters exist in small language models (e.g., Phi-2, TinyLlama) and that a resource-aware, dynamic H2O caching strategy can significantly improve inference throughput and reduce memory pressure on edge hardware without degrading output quality.
proposals.method: We will first profile a suite of SLMs on diverse edge-relevant benchmarks (e.g., instruction following, code generation) to statistically identify and characterize heavy hitters. We will then adapt the H2O algorithm for resource-constrained environments, integrating it with a system akin to BlendServe's resource-aware batching but optimized for intermittent edge compute. The final system will be evaluated on real edge devices (e.g., Raspberry Pi, Jetson Nano) using metrics from Chatbot Arena (human preference correlation) and throughput/latency benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical analysis of heavy hitters in SLMs and deliver an open-source, lightweight caching library for edge inference. It will establish new Pareto frontiers for the cost-quality trade-off for on-device models, directly advancing the field of efficient inference.
proposals.novelty: While H2O is established for large models, applying it to tiny models is non-trivial and unexplored; the caching dynamics, token distributions, and hardware trade-offs are fundamentally different at this scale, making the utility and optimal strategy an open research question.

proposals.title: DualCache: Optimizing LLM Inference Performance Under a Fixed KV Cache Budget
proposals.motivation: The KV cache is a critical bottleneck for the throughput and latency of large language model (LLM) inference, especially for long-context tasks. Prior work, such as cache eviction and compression, focuses on minimizing cache size for a given sequence. However, in production systems, hardware memory is a fixed, scarce resource. The inverse problem—maximizing performance for a hard cache limit—is under-explored and crucial for practical deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a fixed KV cache budget, strategically selecting which tokens to cache—based on a learned, context-aware utility metric—can significantly improve inference quality and throughput compared to naive eviction policies like LRU or uniform attention.
proposals.method: We will formulate the cache allocation as an online optimization problem. Building on Zheng's expertise in resource-aware batching (BlendServe), we will develop a lightweight predictor to estimate the future utility of each token's KV cache entry. This predictor will be trained via reinforcement learning, using reward signals from downstream task performance (e.g., accuracy on QA benchmarks) and system throughput. We will integrate this scheduler into a modified vLLM or Hugging Face Transformers backend, evaluating it against static and heuristic eviction baselines on long-document and multi-turn dialogue benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new framework and practical algorithms for cache-constrained LLM serving. It will deliver a system that makes Pareto-optimal trade-offs between memory, throughput, and output quality for a given hardware budget, directly advancing resource-efficient inference.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior cache minimization work, we treat the cache size as a hard constraint and optimize the *composition* of the cache. This shifts the objective from compression to intelligent allocation, a perspective not systematically studied in pre-2026 LLM systems research.

proposals.title: EddyFlow: Adaptive Query Processing for Dynamic LLM-Based Agent Execution
proposals.motivation: Emergent LLM-based agents and tool-using workflows exhibit non-deterministic, data-dependent control flow, making their execution graphs dynamic and uncertain. Existing orchestration frameworks (e.g., LangChain) rely on static graphs or brittle heuristics, leading to inefficient resource use, high latency, and poor throughput. This problem is critical for scaling complex, multi-step reasoning applications in production.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by modeling an LLM agent's execution as a dynamic query plan and applying adaptive query processing (AQP) techniques, we can build an execution engine that significantly improves throughput and reduces latency through runtime re-optimization of operator ordering, parallelization, and speculative execution.
proposals.method: We will design an execution engine where each LLM call, tool use, or conditional branch is a query operator. Inspired by the Eddy operator, a central router will dynamically reorder operators based on runtime statistics (e.g., token generation rate, tool latency, confidence scores). We will integrate confidence-bound estimation from AQP to trigger speculative execution of likely future branches and employ resource-aware batching (extending BlendServe) to optimize GPU memory and compute across concurrent, heterogeneous queries. The system will be evaluated using modified Chatbot Arena workflows and synthetic benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel execution engine that fundamentally relaxes the need for a predefined static program graph for LLM agents. It will provide the first systematic framework for applying database AQP principles to LLM inference, yielding measurable gains in throughput and latency for dynamic workflows. We will also release a benchmark suite for evaluating adaptive agent execution.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes static LLM inference graphs (e.g., vLLM) or evaluates final outputs (Chatbot Arena), this is the first to treat the emergent execution plan itself as an adaptive query optimization problem, enabling mid-execution re-planning. This fusion of database AQP with LLM systems is a novel, non-obvious direction for improving inference efficiency.

proposals.title: SALI-CACHE: Integrated, Salience-Driven KV Cache Management for Efficient LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: Transformer inference for long contexts is bottlenecked by the quadratic memory and latency overhead of the Key-Value (KV) cache. Existing eviction strategies, like H2O or StreamingLLM, rely on heuristics or static patterns, which are suboptimal and can discard high-utility context, degrading output quality. A principled, low-overhead method to retain only the most salient context is critical for improving throughput and memory efficiency in real-world deployments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a lightweight, learned salience score, computed per KV pair at generation time using its local attention distribution and token features, can accurately predict future utility. By integrating this scoring directly into the attention mechanism to manage a fixed-size priority buffer, we can maintain high model performance while drastically reducing cache memory footprint and eliminating separate eviction prediction latency.
proposals.method: We will modify the transformer's attention block to output a scalar salience score for each generated KV pair, using a tiny MLP over the attention distribution (e.g., entropy, max value) and token embeddings. The cache will be implemented as a priority queue based on these scores. During decoding, new KV pairs are inserted, and the lowest-scoring pair is evicted, a constant-time operation. We will train the salience scorer via reinforcement learning, using a reward that balances output quality (evaluated on benchmarks) against cache compression ratio.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel inference system that unifies attention and cache management, providing a robust, utility-aware alternative to heuristic eviction. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating KV cache efficiency under constrained memory and a practical, open-source implementation for the vLLM or Hugging Face ecosystems, directly advancing the field of efficient LLM serving.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that treats cache eviction as a separate, post-hoc prediction task, our method integrates salience scoring directly into the forward pass, making cache management a seamless, proactive component of the attention mechanism itself, with no additional inference latency.

proposals.title: CacheWave: Adaptive KV Cache Pruning via Attention-Spectral Thresholding
proposals.motivation: The KV cache is a primary bottleneck for long-context LLM inference, consuming prohibitive memory and limiting throughput. Existing methods like static windowing or simple eviction policies are crude and can discard critical context, while full retention is unsustainable. A principled, adaptive method to identify and prune minimally influential cache entries is urgently needed to scale efficient inference.
proposals.hypothesis: The 'influence' of a KV cache entry on future attention can be quantified in a learned latent space, analogous to signal energy in a frequency domain, enabling dynamic, lossy compression with minimal impact on output quality.
proposals.method: We will design a lightweight scoring network that projects KV pairs into a latent 'attention energy' space, using a spectral decomposition (e.g., learned Fourier features) of their cross-attention relationships with recent queries. A dynamic threshold, set by the entropy of the current context window, will determine which entries to prune or compress via low-rank approximation. This system will be integrated into a modified FlashAttention-2 kernel for online evaluation during generation.
