proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, adaptive KV cache management algorithm that significantly reduces memory footprint for long-context inference. We will provide a new benchmark for evaluating cache efficiency versus output fidelity and release optimized kernels to advance the field of high-throughput LLM serving.
proposals.novelty: It inverts the standard cache reuse paradigm by making aggressive, intelligent pruning the default, directly applying signal processing principles of transform-based thresholding—a concept not explored in prior KV cache compression work—to the attention mechanism.

proposals.title: SGLang Backplane: A Runtime-Native Intermediate Representation for Optimized Tool-Augmented LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: Current LLM systems treat external tools and environments as opaque API calls, leading to high latency, poor resource utilization, and brittle agentic workflows. As agent complexity grows, this chained-call paradigm becomes a major bottleneck for throughput and user experience. A runtime-native abstraction is needed to enable deep optimization of these hybrid computation graphs.
proposals.hypothesis: By formally modeling tools and environments as first-class primitives within the SGLang runtime via a unified intermediate representation (IR), we can apply speculative execution, parallel tool invocation, and semantic caching to significantly reduce latency and increase robustness in tool-augmented LLM inference.
proposals.method: We will extend the SGLang runtime to define a Tool-Augmented Computation Graph IR, capturing tool semantics, I/O schemas, and state transitions. The runtime scheduler will use this IR to perform optimizations: 1) Speculatively executing likely tool calls based on LLM generation patterns, 2) Batching and parallelizing independent tool invocations, and 3) Implementing a semantic cache for tool results based on the IR. We will integrate this with SGLang's existing KV cache and RadixAttention for end-to-end optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a coherent programming model and runtime for building efficient, stateful agents, directly lowering latency and improving throughput for tool-use scenarios. It will contribute a novel IR and optimization techniques to the LLM systems literature, bridging the gap between high-level agent frameworks and low-level inference engines.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on API chaining (e.g., LangChain) or simple parallel tool calls, this deeply integrates tool semantics into the core runtime IR, enabling speculative and parallel optimizations that are impossible with opaque wrappers. It applies systems optimization principles from work like BlendServe directly to the emerging problem of agentic workflow efficiency.

proposals.title: SGLang-Orchestra: Adaptive Runtime Orchestration for Dynamic Multi-Tool LLM Programs
proposals.motivation: Current LLM inference systems like SGLang excel at optimizing static computational graphs for high throughput but struggle with dynamic, tool-augmented programs where the execution path depends on intermediate results and live external state. This limits the deployment of complex, interactive LLM agents in real-world applications like robotics control, live data analysis, and adaptive user interfaces, where latency and resource efficiency remain critical bottlenecks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extending SGLang's frontend into an adaptive orchestrator, which uses real-time feedback from program execution and external tool states to make dynamic scheduling decisions, can significantly improve the end-to-end latency and resource utilization of multi-tool LLM programs without sacrificing the throughput benefits of its underlying runtime engine.
proposals.method: We will augment SGLang with a lightweight monitoring layer that tracks LLM generation outputs, tool execution states (e.g., database query latency, simulator readiness), and system resource metrics. This data will feed a reinforcement learning-based scheduler trained to dynamically select, sequence, and batch tool calls, optimizing for a combined objective of low latency and high GPU utilization. The system will integrate a just-in-time graph compiler that can re-optimize execution plans based on runtime feedback, building upon prior work in speculative execution and adaptive batching like BlendServe.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel adaptive orchestration runtime for dynamic LLM programs, 2) a set of benchmarks for evaluating multi-tool LLM system performance beyond static text generation, and 3) open-source integrations demonstrating significant latency reductions in case studies like iterative code execution with REPL tools and interactive data analysis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fusing SGLang's high-throughput, static-graph optimization with dynamic, feedback-driven orchestration, creating a hybrid system that efficiently handles unpredictable, tool-dependent control flows—a capability absent in both pure compilation-based and interpreter-based LLM serving systems.

proposals.title: DAGFusion: A JIT Compilation and Runtime Scheduler for High-Throughput Multi-Modal Inference Pipelines
proposals.motivation: Multi-modal AI pipelines, combining vision, language, and audio models, are becoming standard for complex generation tasks. However, their execution is often a sequential chain of discrete model invocations, suffering from high framework overhead, frequent data movement (e.g., CPU-GPU transfers), and suboptimal hardware utilization, which severely limits throughput and increases latency. Pre-2026 systems like BlendServe optimize single-model batching but do not address the cross-stage inefficiencies inherent in heterogeneous, multi-model pipelines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by statically analyzing a pipeline's computational DAG and applying Just-in-Time (JIT) compilation to fuse compatible adjacent stages into single kernels, we can minimize framework overhead and data transfer latency, leading to significant improvements in end-to-end throughput and latency for multi-modal inference.
proposals.method: We will design a runtime system that first constructs a static DAG from a declarative pipeline specification. A profiler will execute the pipeline to identify critical paths and measure operator costs. A compatibility analyzer, using pre-2026 operator fusion principles from frameworks like TVM and XLA, will determine which adjacent stages (e.g., a vision encoder and a projection layer) can be fused. A JIT compiler will then generate fused, hardware-optimized kernels for these subgraphs. The system will integrate a DAG scheduler to manage the execution of fused and unfused nodes, dynamically adapting to available hardware (GPUs/NPUs).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel runtime system that brings HPC-inspired DAG scheduling and JIT compilation to multi-modal AI pipelines, 2) a set of fusion rules and a compatibility model for heterogeneous AI operators, and 3) empirical evidence of significant throughput and latency improvements on benchmark multi-modal tasks (e.g., image-to-text-to-audio) compared to sequential orchestration frameworks.
proposals.novelty: While JIT compilation and DAG schedulers exist separately, their combined application for dynamically fusing *heterogeneous* models in a multi-modal pipeline is novel, moving beyond single-model optimization (BlendServe) or human evaluation (Chatbot Arena) to address the systemic inefficiencies of composite AI systems.

proposals.title: Vanna: A Learned Query Optimizer for Resource-Aware Multi-Modal Generative Pipelines
proposals.motivation: Multi-modal generative pipelines (e.g., text → image → critique → edit) are becoming common but are executed as static, unoptimized scripts, leading to poor resource utilization and high latency. Pre-2026, systems like BlendServe optimize single-model inference, but lack holistic optimization for dynamic, stochastic DAGs of heterogeneous models. This inefficiency limits the throughput and interactivity of complex AI applications.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling multi-modal pipelines as declarative programs and applying a learned cost model for stochastic modules, a database-inspired optimizer can automatically generate execution plans that significantly improve throughput and latency while preserving output quality, compared to static scheduling.
proposals.method: We will develop Vanna, an execution engine that first profiles generative modules (e.g., diffusion models, LLM critics) to build distributions of latency, cost, and output fidelity. This data trains a learned cost model. We then design a query optimizer that applies transformations like predicate pushdown (early filtering), resource-aware batching inspired by BlendServe, and materialized view planning for caching high-value intermediate representations (e.g., CLIP embeddings). The optimizer uses a dynamic programming search, guided by the cost model, to rewrite pipeline DAGs.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel system for optimizing end-to-end multi-modal generation pipelines. 2) A methodology for learning cost models of stochastic neural modules. 3) Empirical evidence, via a new benchmark of complex pipelines, showing substantial gains in throughput and latency over baseline schedulers.
proposals.novelty: This work is the first to rigorously adapt classical database query optimization—specifically cost-based plan selection and materialized view planning—to the domain of stochastic, multi-modal neural networks, moving beyond single-model inference optimization.

proposals.title: PipeFusion: A Resource-Aware Runtime for Orchestrating Multi-Stage Multi-Modal Generation Pipelines
proposals.motivation: The deployment of complex multi-modal AI systems, such as those combining vision encoders, LLMs, and diffusion models for text-to-image generation, is hindered by inefficient pipeline execution. Pre-2026, these pipelines are often treated as static sequences, leading to suboptimal GPU utilization, high memory overhead, and unpredictable latency due to the heterogeneous compute and memory profiles of different stages. This inefficiency limits the throughput and scalability of real-world multi-modal applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dynamic, resource-aware runtime scheduler can significantly improve the throughput and latency of multi-modal pipelines by intelligently batching requests across pipeline stages and managing intermediate feature caches, without compromising output quality.
proposals.method: We will develop PipeFusion, extending the resource-aware batching principles from BlendServe to multi-modal DAGs. The core is a scheduler that profiles the compute/memory footprint of each stage (e.g., CLIP, LLaMA, Stable Diffusion). It will perform dynamic, cross-stage batch sizing and reordering using a cost model to minimize makespan. We will implement a shared, compressed KV cache for common intermediate representations (e.g., text embeddings) across requests and explore selective recomputation strategies to trade memory for compute, optimizing for total cost of inference.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic framework for optimizing end-to-end multi-modal inference, delivering measurable improvements in throughput and cost-efficiency. We will release an open-source system and a new benchmark suite for evaluating multi-modal pipeline performance, filling a gap in current LLM-focused benchmarking like Chatbot Arena.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes single-model inference or linear text pipelines, PipeFusion introduces a holistic scheduler for heterogeneous multi-stage DAGs, dynamically co-optimizing batching, caching, and memory management across fundamentally different model architectures.

proposals.title: LoopOpt: Compiler-Inspired Loop Optimizations for Latency and Cost Reduction in Language Model Execution Graphs
proposals.motivation: The rise of agentic workflows and complex reasoning chains (e.g., ReAct, iterative refinement) creates execution graphs with repetitive, costly LLM calls, directly impacting inference latency and operational cost. Current systems like BlendServe optimize batch-level resource utilization but lack a principled, graph-level optimizer to eliminate redundant computations within these logical 'loops'. A systematic approach to optimize such graphs is critical for scalable and efficient LLM applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formalizing repetitive structures in LM execution graphs as loops and applying adapted compiler optimization passes (e.g., invariant code motion, fusion) can significantly reduce the number of required LLM calls and intermediate steps without altering the semantic output, thereby lowering latency and cost.
proposals.method: We will design a static graph optimizer that first analyzes a high-level LM program graph (e.g., defined via frameworks like LangChain or DSPy) to identify loop patterns and data dependencies. We will then implement optimization passes: 1) **Invariant Code Motion** to hoist static sub-prompts outside loops, 2) **Loop Fusion** to merge adjacent refinement steps with compatible contexts, and 3) **Controlled Unrolling** for fixed, small iterations. The optimized graph will be executed via a runtime that integrates with existing batching systems (inspired by BlendServe) for hardware-aware dispatch. We will evaluate using benchmarks from Chatbot Arena and custom agentic workflows.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel graph optimization framework for LM-based programs, delivering measurable reductions in token usage and latency. It will also provide a formal analogy between compiler loops and LM execution patterns, opening a new direction for inference systems research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes inference for single prompts or batch throughput, applying classical compiler loop optimizations to the logical structure of LM programs is a novel cross-disciplinary analogy. This moves optimization upstream from the kernel level to the algorithmic workflow level.

proposals.title: StreamFlow: A Cost-Based Adaptive Execution Planner for Continuous Language Model Agent Programs
proposals.motivation: Long-running LM agent programs, which perform multi-step reasoning and tool use, are increasingly deployed for continuous tasks like monitoring and interactive assistance. Current execution engines treat these programs as static scripts, leading to inefficient resource use and high latency due to unpredictable tool performance and data arrival. A principled approach to optimize their execution in real-time, akin to database query optimization for streaming data, is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling a continuous LM agent program as a dynamic dataflow graph over infinite streams and applying cost-based optimization with runtime statistics, an adaptive execution planner can significantly improve throughput and reduce latency while maintaining correctness.
proposals.method: We will design a Stream-Optimized Execution Planner that: 1) compiles agent programs (e.g., LangChain/LLamaIndex workflows) into an initial dataflow graph of operators (LLM calls, tool invocations, conditionals); 2) instruments runtime to collect statistics on token generation speed, tool latency, and stream window properties; 3) implements a cost model and a continuous query optimizer (inspired by Apache Calcite and Flink) to perform live plan rewrites, adapting strategies like switching between eager/lazy execution, reordering sub-tasks, and caching intermediate results across loop iterations based on changing costs.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel execution engine for LM agents that dynamically optimizes for throughput and latency, a formalization of agent programs as streaming dataflows, and open-source benchmarks demonstrating performance gains on continuous tasks like real-time data analysis and persistent chatbots.
proposals.novelty: This is the first work to fuse database-style cost-based query optimization and stream processing semantics directly into the execution core of LM agent programs, enabling live adaptive rewrites rather than static compilation or heuristic batching.

proposals.title: StreamIR: A Live Dataflow Compiler for Incremental Optimization of LLM Agents
proposals.motivation: LLM agents are increasingly deployed for long-running, interactive tasks, but their execution loops—involving tools, memory, and reasoning—are often implemented as monolithic, static scripts. This leads to inefficient resource use, high latency, and an inability to adapt to changing runtime conditions. Pre-2026 systems like BlendServe optimize offline inference but lack the dynamic, incremental optimization needed for live agents.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling an LLM agent's components as operators in a stream-aware intermediate representation (IR), we can apply compiler optimizations incrementally to the live dataflow, thereby reducing end-to-end latency and computational cost without halting the agent or sacrificing correctness.
proposals.method: We will design a StreamIR that lifts an agent's Python program into a dataflow graph where nodes are tools, LLM calls, and memory operations, and edges are streams of tokens, function arguments, and state updates. We will implement optimization passes (e.g., speculative tool fusion, state caching, and adaptive batching) that trigger on graph updates or performance metrics. The system will be integrated with vLLM's efficient KV cache management and evaluated using Chatbot Arena's human preference framework and latency/cost benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel compiler framework that enables real-time optimization of live LLM agents, a set of stream-aware compiler passes tailored for agentic workloads, and empirical evidence showing reduced latency and cost on interactive benchmarks. It bridges the gap between static inference optimization and dynamic agent execution.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on static model serving or offline agent compilation, our approach introduces a live, stream-processing semantics to agent IR, enabling incremental optimizations that react to runtime data and agent state evolution, a capability absent in pre-2026 systems.

proposals.title: FusionDyno: Adaptive Kernel Fusion for Dynamic LLM Graphs via Lightweight Profiling and Constrained Search
proposals.motivation: Kernel fusion is a critical optimization for LLM inference throughput and memory efficiency, but existing methods (e.g., in frameworks like TVM, Triton) rely on static graph analysis. This fails for the dynamic execution graphs of modern LLMs, where operator patterns, memory access, and data dependencies vary significantly with input sequence length, batch size, and model architecture (e.g., mixture-of-experts). A static fusion strategy optimized for one workload can degrade performance or even break for another, limiting robust deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a lightweight, profile-guided compiler can automatically discover and generate near-optimal kernel fusion plans for a given LLM and workload distribution, outperforming static fusion strategies in throughput and memory efficiency across diverse inference scenarios.
proposals.method: We will build a system with a two-phase compiler. First, a lightweight profiler executes representative sample batches to trace the dynamic operator graph and collect fine-grained memory access patterns. Second, a compiler uses this profile to formulate fusion as a constrained combinatorial optimization problem, searching over potential fusion regions with a cost model incorporating kernel launch overhead, register pressure, and shared memory usage. It will generate specialized CUDA kernels via a template-based code generator. The system will be integrated with and evaluated against state-of-the-art serving systems like vLLM and TGI.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source adaptive fusion compiler that automatically improves LLM inference throughput and reduces memory footprint for variable workloads. It will provide the first systematic study of dynamic graph behaviors' impact on fusion efficacy and establish a benchmark for profile-guided compilation in LLM serving.
proposals.novelty: Unlike static fusion compilers, our method dynamically adapts fusion strategies to observed runtime behavior. It moves beyond hand-tuned fusion rules or architecture-specific optimizations to a general, automated approach for dynamic graphs.

proposals.title: SprintServe: Coordinated Prefill, Scheduling, and Cache Management for Minimal Time-to-First-Token in Interactive LLMs
proposals.motivation: In interactive LLM applications like chatbots and copilots, user-perceived latency, dominated by time-to-first-token (TTFT), is critical for engagement and usability. Existing systems like vLLM and Orca focus heavily on high-throughput decoding and batch scheduling, often treating the initial prompt processing (prefill) as a secondary concern. This creates a bottleneck where high-priority interactive queries are blocked behind long-running prefills from other requests, degrading the user experience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a holistic, system-level optimization coordinating speculative prefill execution, adaptive prefill scheduling, and latency-aware KV cache management can significantly reduce TTFT for interactive single-query workloads, even at a marginal cost to aggregate throughput.
proposals.method: We will design SprintServe, a three-component system: 1) A speculative prefill engine that uses a small, fast draft model to predict initial KV states for common prompt prefixes, validated by the full model. 2) An adaptive scheduler that dynamically interrupts and interleaves long-running prefills to prioritize TTFT-critical requests, inspired by OS preemption. 3) A latency-aware KV cache manager that proactively allocates and retains cache for high-priority interactive sessions, trading eviction efficiency for reduced prefill cost on follow-up turns. We will evaluate end-to-end performance using interactive benchmarks derived from Chatbot Arena traces.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, open-source serving system optimized for interactive TTFT, a detailed characterization of the latency-throughput trade-off for single-query workloads, and new metrics for evaluating perceived latency in LLM serving. It shifts the optimization focus from pure throughput to user-centric latency.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes individual components (e.g., PagedAttention for cache, continuous batching for throughput), SprintServe is the first to holistically coordinate speculative prefill, preemptive scheduling, and cache management specifically for TTFT, treating idle compute during context ingestion as a primary resource to optimize.

proposals.title: L3GO: Learning a Latency-Cost Model for Latency-Aware Graph Optimization in Single-Query LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: Current LLM inference systems, like those optimized in BlendServe, prioritize batch throughput for offline scenarios, leaving interactive, single-query latency suboptimal. Techniques for kernel fusion and memory optimization are applied statically, while latency-critical innovations like RadixAttention operate in isolation without considering their holistic impact on the end-to-end computational graph. A system that can dynamically reconfigure the execution plan to minimize single-query latency is crucial for real-time conversational AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a learned cost model can accurately predict the end-to-end latency impact of graph-level transformations (e.g., fusion, kernel selection, memory layout), and that using this model to drive a graph rewriting system will yield significant latency reductions for single-query inference compared to static or throughput-optimized strategies.
proposals.method: We will develop L3GO, a two-stage system. First, we train a graph neural network (GNN)-based cost model on profiled execution traces of varied computational graphs (from PyTorch/XLA or ONNX) to predict total forward-pass latency. Second, we integrate this model into a graph rewriting engine that explores a space of transformations—including a latency-optimized RadixAttention variant, operator fusion, and memory layout changes—using a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) algorithm to select the lowest-latency graph for a given model and hardware configuration.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, learnable latency-cost model for LLM computational graphs, 2) a dynamic graph optimization framework that holistically minimizes single-query latency, and 3) an open-source implementation and benchmark demonstrating latency improvements on models like Llama-2 and Vicuna in interactive settings.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of a learned, high-level graph optimization policy with low-level, latency-critical attention mechanisms, creating a closed-loop system that optimizes for end-to-end interactive latency—a goal distinct from and more challenging than aggregate throughput optimization.

proposals.title: Low-Latency LLM Inference via Dynamic Cache Management and Query-Aware Scheduling
proposals.motivation: Current LLM serving systems, including those using RadixAttention, are heavily optimized for high-throughput batch processing, which introduces significant latency for single, interactive queries. This is a critical bottleneck for real-time applications like conversational AI, coding assistants, and interactive analysis. A dedicated focus on latency optimization, rather than just throughput, is essential for improving user experience in these interactive scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by redesigning KV cache management and inference scheduling to prioritize single-query latency, we can achieve significant reductions in Time to First Token (TTFT) and inter-token latency without compromising model quality, outperforming throughput-optimized baselines in interactive settings.
proposals.method: We will adapt the RadixAttention mechanism to maintain a dynamic, query-aware prefix cache that prioritizes eviction of less relevant cached sequences to minimize memory bandwidth pressure for a single stream. We will integrate this with a novel, lightweight scheduler that bypasses traditional batching logic for interactive queries, employing speculative execution with a small draft model and fast verification. The system will be evaluated using a modified version of the Chatbot Arena framework to measure human-perceived latency.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new serving system architecture and a set of algorithms specifically designed for low-latency, single-query LLM inference. It will provide a comprehensive benchmark for latency in interactive settings, moving beyond aggregate throughput metrics. The resulting open-source system will enable more responsive LLM applications.
proposals.novelty: While RadixAttention improves cache hit rates for batches, its application is not optimized for the unique memory access patterns and scheduling constraints of a single query. Our approach novelly co-designs cache management and scheduling from a first-principles latency perspective, a departure from the throughput-centric adaptations of existing methods.

proposals.title: PortPrompt: Adaptive Prompt Portfolios for Robust and High-Throughput LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: As LLMs are deployed for massive-batch tasks like content moderation, data labeling, and code generation, a single, static prompt becomes a critical reliability risk. Systematic prompt failures, as seen in vacillation studies, can lead to large-scale, costly errors. Current inference optimization focuses on throughput (e.g., via batching and caching) but lacks mechanisms for in-production prompt robustness, creating a gap between efficiency and reliability.
proposals.hypothesis: An adaptive system that empirically selects an optimal prompt from a diverse portfolio using a small validation batch, and monitors for degradation during large-scale inference, will significantly reduce systematic error rates while maintaining high throughput compared to monolithic prompt strategies.
proposals.method: We will design a two-stage system. First, a portfolio generator creates prompt variants via systematic paraphrasing, few-shot example selection, and instruction restructuring. Second, a runtime manager, inspired by resource-aware batching (BlendServe), executes a small validation batch through all variants, scoring them on metrics like consistency (Ask Again, Then Fail) and human preference alignment (Chatbot Arena). The top variant is deployed for the full batch, with continuous statistical process control on sampled outputs to trigger fallback to a secondary portfolio member or a human review halt.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a framework for robust, production-ready LLM inference that explicitly manages prompt fragility as a resource. It will contribute new benchmarks for prompt stability under massive batch processing and open-source a system that integrates reliability sampling with high-throughput inference pipelines.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes inference latency or evaluates final model quality, PortPrompt uniquely treats prompts as a portfolio of fallible strategies to be actively managed, introducing adaptive selection and degradation monitoring as core components of the inference stack.

proposals.title: Federated Prompt Execution: A Resource-Aware Orchestrator for Massive-Scale LLM Inference on Heterogeneous Edge Devices
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of edge devices and mobile LLMs creates a vast, underutilized computational fabric, but current inference systems are designed for data-center homogeneity. Executing complex prompts over globally distributed, private data requires a new paradigm that addresses extreme device heterogeneity, network churn, and resource constraints, which existing federated learning or centralized inference frameworks cannot efficiently handle.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single complex prompt can be compiled into a resource-aware, fault-tolerant execution plan and distributed across millions of heterogeneous edge devices, achieving higher aggregate throughput and enabling new privacy-preserving applications compared to centralized cloud inference.
proposals.method: We will design a central orchestrator that extends concepts from BlendServe's resource-aware batching to a federated setting, compiling prompts into computational graphs. A lightweight runtime client, inspired by mobile LLM optimizations, will execute sub-graphs using efficient KV cache management for local data. The orchestrator will use a device capability benchmark (extending Chatbot Arena's evaluation logic) to profile devices and manage job distribution, incorporating redundancy and checkpointing to handle churn. Result aggregation will include mechanisms to detect and mitigate inference inconsistencies observed in works like 'Ask Again, Then Fail'.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel system architecture for federated prompt execution, 2) new scheduling algorithms for massive, heterogeneous edge inference, and 3) an open-source framework and benchmark for evaluating large-scale edge LLM systems. It directly advances the fields of efficient inference and federated computation.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in fusing the concepts of prompt-as-program, federated computation, and high-throughput inference orchestration into a single system designed for planetary-scale edge device fleets, moving beyond federated learning for model training to federated execution for inference.

proposals.title: FlashBatch: A Memory-Efficient, High-Throughput System for Massive Single-Prompt LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: The deployment of large language models (LLMs) for large-scale data processing tasks—such as sentiment analysis, data labeling, or content moderation across millions of documents—is bottlenecked by memory and throughput inefficiencies. Current batching strategies, like those in BlendServe, are optimized for diverse prompts, not the unique scenario of a single, potentially complex prompt applied to a massive, homogeneous dataset. This leads to suboptimal GPU utilization and high memory overhead from redundant prompt state storage.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing a novel inference system that treats the static prompt as a persistent, shareable computational graph and optimizes data loading and attention caching specifically for this workload, we can achieve near-linear throughput scaling and drastically reduce per-instance memory footprint for massive batch, single-prompt jobs.
proposals.method: We will extend the KV cache mechanism to create a persistent, read-only 'Prompt Cache' that is computed once and shared across all batch items, eliminating redundant computation. We will develop a resource-aware, staged batching scheduler that pipelines token generation with non-blocking I/O for the variable-length data inputs. The system will integrate a novel attention kernel that bypasses recomputation for the static prompt prefix and employs selective paging of per-instance KV caches to host memory, informed by the memory management principles in vLLM, to handle batches an order of magnitude larger than current systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new open-source inference system capable of unprecedented throughput for a critical class of LLM workloads. It will provide a formal model and benchmark for single-prompt massive batch inference, filling a gap in current evaluation suites like Chatbot Arena. The research will also yield insights into memory-sharing optimizations that could benefit broader inference scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes for mixed prompts, this is the first system designed from the ground up for the single-prompt, massive-data regime, transforming the prompt from a per-request cost to a fixed, amortized overhead. The integration of a persistent prompt cache with staged batching and aggressive KV cache paging is a novel architectural approach not explored in existing batching frameworks.
