ideas.title: Budget-Aware, Multi-Stage Compilation with Performance Guarantees
ideas.description: This compiler design accepts a user-defined performance budget relative to a fully optimized baseline as a first-class constraint. It operates in distinct stages: a first stage uses minimal optimization for near-instantaneous code generation during active development, providing rapid feedback. A second, asynchronous stage applies selective, costlier optimizations only to code hotspots or until the performance budget is met, ensuring the final output never exceeds a specified runtime penalty. This approach guarantees that compilation speed is maximized *within* the bounds of acceptable application performance, making it practical for real-world, performance-sensitive development workflows.

ideas.title: Scale down to instruction-tuning contamination
ideas.description: Analyze contamination specifically within instruction or conversation fine-tuning datasets.
ideas.sourceTitle: Rethinking Benchmark and Contamination for Language Models with Rephrased Samples

ideas.title: Predictability-Aware Adaptive Control Flow Management with Performance Guarantees
ideas.description: This system addresses dynamic control flow by first analyzing programs to classify their control flow patterns along a predictability spectrum. It employs a multi-tiered execution engine that generates optimized native code for predictable dynamic branches while maintaining efficient interpretation capabilities for unpredictable paths. The framework provides statistical performance guarantees by modeling control flow behavior and selecting execution strategies that bound worst-case latency. This approach ensures efficient handling of data-dependent control flow while maintaining predictable performance characteristics suitable for real-time systems.

ideas.title: Apply cryptographic hashing for exact match detection
ideas.description: Use cryptographic hash functions for efficient and robust exact-match detection in large datasets.
ideas.sourceTitle: Rethinking Benchmark and Contamination for Language Models with Rephrased Samples

ideas.title: Continuous Adversarial Auditing for Dynamic Dataset Contamination
ideas.description: This research establishes a living framework for contamination detection by formalizing a continuous adversarial audit cycle. Instead of a fixed standard, the system maintains a rotating panel of adversarial 'red team' models that are incentivized to discover new forms of data contamination or leakage, such as subtle memorization or task-specific shortcuts. Their findings are used to generate new benchmarks and update decontamination protocols, creating a dynamic defense that adapts to emerging threats. This approach treats contamination as an evolving adversary rather than a static property, ensuring detection mechanisms remain robust over time and across model generations.

ideas.title: Extend learning-based cost model to more hardware
ideas.description: Apply the learning-based cost modeling approach to a wider variety of hardware targets beyond CPUs, GPUs, and FPGAs.
ideas.sourceTitle: TVM: An Automated End-to-End Optimizing Compiler for Deep Learning

ideas.title: Integration with continuous batching optimizations
ideas.description: Combine PagedAttention with advanced continuous batching strategies for further latency and throughput improvements.
ideas.sourceTitle: Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention

ideas.title: Inverse: A decompiler from traces to SGLang
ideas.description: Build a tool that converts low-level LM execution traces back into high-level SGLang programs.
ideas.sourceTitle: SGLang: Efficient Execution of Structured Language Model Programs

ideas.title: Transferring Cost Models via Meta-Learning and Invariant Feature Spaces
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to improve the transfer of learned cost models across different operators and neural networks by drawing an analogy from computational biology. In protein engineering, predicting properties for novel proteins is achieved by meta-learning on diverse families to find invariant biophysical principles in a latent space. Adapting this solution, we will develop methods that use meta-learning on cost data from a wide variety of source workloads to discover a shared, low-dimensional representation of fundamental computational primitives. This invariant feature space would allow a cost model for a new target workload to be initialized effectively from this representation and refined with minimal target-specific data, solving the core transfer challenge.

ideas.title: Incorporate program synthesis techniques
ideas.description: Integrate formal program synthesis to generate correct-by-construction tensor program variants.
ideas.sourceTitle: Learning to Optimize Tensor Programs

ideas.title: Adaptive Query Planning for Dynamic Language Model Execution Graphs
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel execution engine for language model programs (e.g., agent networks, tool-using workflows) that treats the evolving, uncertain program structure as a dynamic query execution plan. By adapting techniques from adaptive query processing in databases—such as mid-query re-optimization, eddies, and confidence-bound estimation—the system can make runtime decisions about parallelization, operator ordering, and speculative execution without a predefined static graph. This enables efficient execution of programs where the control flow, data dependencies, and even the set of required tools or reasoning steps are discovered or change during execution itself, fundamentally relaxing the assumption of a known, fixed program structure.

ideas.title: Optimize for multi-modal pipeline execution
ideas.description: Further develop runtime optimizations for complex, multi-stage multi-modal generation pipelines.
ideas.sourceTitle: SGLang: Efficient Execution of Structured Language Model Programs

ideas.title: OS-Inspired Memory Paging for Dynamic Model Unlearning and Contextual Integrity
ideas.description: This research proposes adapting operating system paging algorithms (like LRU, clock, or prefetching) to manage the activation and suppression of a neural network's internal representations. Instead of swapping model layers to disk, the system would 'page out' or de-emphasize specific latent knowledge patterns—particularly those corresponding to memorized benchmark data—during evaluation to prevent contamination. Conversely, it could 'page in' or reinforce relevant, non-memorized knowledge for a given task. This creates a novel paradigm for dynamic, context-aware model unlearning and integrity maintenance without costly retraining.

ideas.title: Adapting Signal Processing Windowing & Filtering for Incremental LM Agent State Updates
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from Digital Signal Processing (DSP) to the problem of continuous, incremental execution in LM agent loops. The analogous DSP solution involves processing continuous data streams in real-time using sliding windows (e.g., Hamming, Hann) and digital filters (FIR, IIR), which apply transformations to recent signal segments to efficiently update outputs without full recomputation. Adapted to the research context, an LM agent's interaction history is treated as a temporal signal. A configurable sliding window with tapering functions selects and weights recent 'frames' of dialogue, actions, or observations. A lightweight, potentially learnable filter mechanism then incrementally updates a compressed agent state or context summary from this windowed input. This approach provides stream processing semantics for the agent loop, allowing for continuous operation where state evolves incrementally with new inputs, minimizing computational overhead and latency by avoiding reprocessing of the entire history at each step.

ideas.title: Synthesizing Tensor Programs via Chemical Reaction Network Design Principles
ideas.description: This idea adapts formal synthesis techniques from synthetic biology, specifically the automated design of Chemical Reaction Networks (CRNs) from high-level functional specifications, to the domain of tensor program generation. In the analogous field, tools take specifications (e.g., produce target molecule) and synthesize a network of biochemical reactions guaranteed to satisfy properties like mass conservation. Adapted to this context, we propose a system where a formal specification for a tensor computation—including output shape, algebraic invariants, and precision bounds—is provided alongside a library of pre-verified tensor operation 'motifs' (e.g., fused multiply-add patterns, tiling templates, reduction schemes). A program synthesis engine then searches the combinatorial space of motif assemblies to generate a complete tensor program that is correct-by-construction, inherently guaranteeing shape consistency, algebraic equivalence to the specification, and adherence to specified numerical properties, thereby automating the creation of provably correct tensor program variants.

ideas.title: Establishing a Contamination Tolerance Curve for Benchmarking
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that benchmark datasets must be perfectly decontaminated to be useful. By drawing an analogy from microbiology and food safety—where quantitative risk assessment defines acceptable contamination levels rather than demanding sterility—we adapt the solution to AI evaluation. The analogous solution uses dose-response models to set safety thresholds; here, we would empirically establish 'contamination tolerance curves' for benchmarks. This involves systematically introducing controlled, measurable contamination (e.g., data leakage, memorized examples, or distributional shifts) into a benchmark and modeling its impact on the performance rankings of a suite of models. The goal is to determine the threshold of contamination that significantly skews results, thereby providing a practical, evidence-based standard for how 'clean' a benchmark needs to be for its conclusions to remain valid.

ideas.title: Integrate with more external tools and environments
ideas.description: Enhance SGLang's frontend for seamless integration with diverse external tools and interactive environments.
ideas.sourceTitle: SGLang: Efficient Execution of Structured Language Model Programs

ideas.title: Applying Gene Regulatory Network Analysis to LM Intermediate Representations
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution methods from computational biology's analysis of gene regulatory networks (GRNs) to the domain of language model compiler intermediate representations (IRs). In the analogous field, GRNs are analyzed using techniques like network motif discovery, perturbation simulation, and modular decomposition to understand function, identify control points, and predict intervention outcomes. The adapted research idea applies this methodology by treating a LM's IR dataflow graph as a computational regulatory network. Nodes represent operations or layers, and edges represent dataflow. By applying GRN-inspired analysis—searching for significant computational motifs, simulating perturbations to find critical components, and decomposing the graph into functional modules—we can gain a systematic, structural understanding of LM programs. This enables new forms of analysis for model interpretation, more informed model editing, and optimizations that preserve functional integrity, analogous to how GRN analysis guides biological engineering.

ideas.title: Constraint-Guided Synthesis with Active Adversarial Critique
ideas.description: This system generates program variants through a synthesis engine that is initially unconstrained by templates but is immediately governed by a dynamic, adversarial constraint layer. This layer employs learned critic models and formal verification tools to evaluate each candidate variant against core objectives like functional correctness, efficiency, and security. Variants that pass this critique are used to refine the generator, creating a feedback loop that prioritizes robust novelty over pattern replication. The result is a generative process that discovers viable program structures without prior templates while being actively steered away from the latent flaws of its training corpus.

ideas.title: Temporal and Spatial Scheduling for Multi-Tenant Hardware Inspired by Air Traffic Control
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of dedicated hardware access by drawing an analogy from air traffic control systems, which expertly manage multiple entities sharing a constrained, high-stakes resource. In air traffic control, safety and efficiency are maintained through temporal separation (scheduling), spatial separation (resource partitioning), and dynamic prioritization, all monitored in real-time. Adapted to multi-tenant shared hardware, this translates to a new scheduling and resource management paradigm. The research would develop a framework that treats hardware components as a navigable space, using advanced, predictive scheduling to assign precise temporal windows of execution to tenants and logically partition spatial resources (e.g., specific cache ways, memory controllers, execution units) to minimize interference. The system would integrate continuous hardware telemetry with policy-based priority rules, enabling safe, efficient, and performant co-execution of diverse workloads without requiring dedicated access, much like air traffic controllers safely guide numerous aircraft through shared skies and runways.

ideas.title: Create a contamination benchmark suite
ideas.description: Build a standardized set of contaminated samples to evaluate decontamination tool effectiveness.
ideas.sourceTitle: Rethinking Benchmark and Contamination for Language Models with Rephrased Samples

ideas.title: Holistic Time-to-First-Token Optimization for Interactive LLMs
ideas.description: This research proposes a system designed to minimize the total latency, specifically time-to-first-token (TTFT), for interactive single-query LLM interactions. It moves beyond optimizing just the decoding phase by introducing a coordinated three-component architecture: a speculative prefill engine to accelerate initial prompt processing, an adaptive scheduler to minimize idle compute during context ingestion, and a latency-aware KV cache manager. The system is evaluated end-to-end on interactive benchmarks, dynamically trading off marginal throughput to achieve the lowest possible perceived latency for a user.

ideas.title: Contamination-Resilient Hardware Co-Design via Learned Cost Models
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using learned program cost models to generate hardware architectures that are optimized for a world where data contamination is assumed to be unavoidable and potentially beneficial. Instead of designing hardware for idealized, clean benchmarks, the cost models would be trained on or evaluated against workloads containing controlled, characterized contamination (e.g., synthetic noise, distribution shifts, or adversarial patterns). The goal is to co-design hardware that exhibits inherent resilience to these imperfections, potentially by discovering architectural features that improve model calibration or out-of-distribution generalization when running on the contaminated data it was optimized for. This creates a closed-loop system where understanding data pathologies directly informs the creation of more robust and efficient physical computing substrates.

ideas.title: Contamination-Aware Dual Learning for Pruning Instruction-Tuning Data
ideas.description: This research direction trains a model to act as a dual-process critic, learning to predict suboptimal or inefficient implementations of instructions (e.g., code, reasoning steps, or structured outputs). This critic model is then applied to analyze instruction-tuning datasets, flagging examples where the provided 'gold' response exhibits these predictable inefficiencies, which are strong indicators of data contamination or low-quality synthetic data. By pruning this contaminated subspace from the training data, the final instruction-tuned model avoids learning from these flawed examples, leading to more robust and genuinely capable performance. This creates a self-improving loop where the model's understanding of suboptimality directly cleanses the data it learns from.

ideas.title: Analyze longitudinal user behavior and adaptation
ideas.description: Study how individual users change their interaction strategies with LLMs over multiple sessions.
ideas.sourceTitle: LMSYS-Chat-1M: A Large-Scale Real-World LLM Conversation Dataset

ideas.title: Challenge single, centralized runtime assumption
ideas.description: Design a distributed version of SGLang for execution across multiple, heterogeneous devices or clusters.
ideas.sourceTitle: SGLang: Efficient Execution of Structured Language Model Programs

ideas.title: Template-Free, User-Adaptive LLM Interaction Protocols
ideas.description: This research inverts the standard paradigm by having an LLM system learn to generate effective interaction protocols (e.g., prompting strategies, task decomposition methods) without starting from any fixed template. It achieves this by longitudinally analyzing how a specific user's behavior, success, and adaptation patterns evolve across many sessions. The synergy emerges as the system uses this longitudinal behavioral analysis not to select from a menu of known strategies, but to synthesize and iteratively refine novel, user-tailored protocols from first principles, creating a fully adaptive and co-evolutionary human-LLM partnership.

ideas.title: Apply Cryptographic Hashing with Content-Addressable Storage for Exact-Match Detection
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core mechanism of Distributed Version Control Systems (like Git) for exact-match detection in large datasets. In Git, cryptographic hashing creates a content-addressable storage system where objects are identified and retrieved by their hash, enabling efficient deduplication and integrity checks. The adapted solution applies this principle to generic data management: compute a robust cryptographic hash (e.g., SHA-256) for each data unit (record, file, or block) and use it as a unique, content-derived key. An index mapping hashes to storage locations allows for instantaneous exact-match detection via a hash lookup, dramatically improving efficiency over traditional comparison methods. This enables robust applications in data deduplication, change tracking, plagiarism detection, and ensuring data consistency across distributed systems, all while providing strong guarantees against collisions.

ideas.title: Massive Batch Prompt Processing via High-Throughput Screening Architecture
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from Pharmaceutical High-Throughput Screening (HTS) to optimize systems for running one complex prompt across millions of data inputs. In HTS, millions of chemical compounds are processed through standardized biological assays using automated liquid handlers, microplate arrays, and parallel detection. The adapted idea conceptualizes the complex prompt as a fixed assay protocol and each data input as a compound in a well. The system design involves: 1) Structuring inputs into optimized batches ('prompt plates') for efficient loading, 2) Implementing an automated 'context injection' mechanism (analogous to reagent dispensers) to consistently combine the prompt with each input, 3) Parallelized execution across GPU arrays with scheduling that accounts for variable input complexity and resource load, and 4) Dedicated output pipelines for collating, parsing, and performing initial analysis on the massive batch of structured responses, ensuring scalability and reproducibility for single-prompt, massive-batch jobs.

ideas.title: Modality-Aware Semantic Decontamination for Multimodal Training Corpora
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for decontaminating multimodal datasets by explicitly modeling how semantic contamination manifests differently across data types. The system uses a shared multimodal encoder to project text, images, and code into a unified semantic space where a contamination concept (e.g., 'copyrighted content') is defined. It then trains small, efficient modality-specific networks to detect the distinct statistical signatures of that concept within each data type's native feature distribution. This approach allows for the targeted removal of problematic content from large-scale, heterogeneous training sets while preserving clean data, ultimately producing safer and more legally compliant multimodal models.

ideas.title: Hardware-Aware Contamination for Calibration-Aware Primitives
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a novel methodology where, instead of generating hardware primitives solely from clean workload traces, the system intentionally contaminates training or calibration data with controlled, structured noise and distributional shifts that mimic potential hardware-level approximations or errors. The resulting changes in model behavior, particularly in calibration metrics and generalization gaps, are analyzed to identify which computational patterns are most sensitive or robust to such 'contamination'. This sensitivity profile is then fed into the inverse hardware design process to automatically propose new tensor primitives and architectural features that are explicitly optimized not just for performance, but for maintaining model calibration and reliability under realistic, non-ideal data conditions, thereby co-designing robust hardware and robust models.

ideas.title: Optimize for dynamic shape models
ideas.description: Extend TensorIR's optimization capabilities to efficiently handle models with dynamic or data-dependent tensor shapes.
ideas.sourceTitle: TensorIR: An Abstraction for Automatic Tensorized Program Optimization

ideas.title: Provenance-Aware, Multi-Model Verification for Robust LLM Training Data Decontamination
ideas.description: This research designs a decontamination system that overcomes the circularity of using a single advanced LLM. It establishes a verification panel of architecturally diverse models and knowledge sources to independently audit training data for memorized or copyrighted content. Flagged data is then processed through a provenance-aware module that rewrites or masks problematic segments while preserving factual meaning, using traceable edits rather than opaque regeneration. This ensures the decontamination process itself is auditable and does not rely on a potentially contaminated single model's judgment.

ideas.title: Content-Addressable Distributed KV Cache with Cryptographic Coherence
ideas.description: This research fuses cryptographic hashing with distributed shared memory concepts to create a novel content-addressable architecture for distributed Key-Value (KV) caches, such as those used in large language model inference. Each unique cache entry (e.g., an attention key/value vector block) is identified by its cryptographic hash, which serves as its global address within a distributed shared memory space. This allows nodes to request, share, and page cache content based on these hashes, automatically deduplicating identical data across the system and providing built-in integrity verification. The synergy creates a robust, efficient caching layer where coherence and data retrieval are managed through hash-based protocols, significantly reducing network bandwidth and storage requirements while ensuring consistency even in partially trusted environments.

ideas.title: Distributed Contamination Benchmarking for Heterogeneous Decontamination Pipelines
ideas.description: This research creates a benchmark suite for evaluating LLM training data decontamination tools, designed explicitly for execution across heterogeneous, distributed GPU clusters (e.g., mixes of different GPU generations, memory sizes, and network interconnects). The benchmark includes standardized contaminated datasets and a framework that measures both the final model's purity and the decontamination pipeline's computational metrics—like throughput, latency, and resource utilization across fragmented hardware. This enables the community to develop and compare decontamination methods that are not only effective but also practically deployable in real-world, resource-constrained research and industry settings.

ideas.title: Auditing for Contamination in Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Learning Systems
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel audit framework where a population of heterogeneous learning agents (e.g., with different model architectures, inductive biases, or training algorithms) is trained on or interacts with a synthetic dataset. The core investigation is to systematically analyze how hidden benchmark contamination from the dataset generator manifests differently across the heterogeneous agent population. This approach moves beyond detecting if contamination exists to diagnosing how it asymmetrically affects learning, which agent architectures are most susceptible, and whether contaminated knowledge becomes concentrated or distorted in specific sub-populations, creating new systemic evaluation biases.

ideas.title: Neural Compiler: Graph Rewriting via Learned, Domain-Specific Heuristics
ideas.description: This idea proposes a hybrid 'Neural Compiler' for computational graph optimization, drawing an analogy from bioinformatics' protein folding solutions. In protein folding, learned models predict constraints to guide a traditional search algorithm. Adapted here, the learned tensor optimizer is trained to predict high-level properties—such as the potential benefit of fusing a sequence of operations or the suitability of a subgraph for a specific kernel rewrite—generating 'rewrite potentials' and compatibility scores. These learned heuristics act as a guide for a deterministic, rule-based graph rewriting engine, directing it to prioritize the most promising transformations within the vast space of possible graph rewrites. This combines the pattern recognition and predictive power of a learned model with the precision, verifiability, and robustness of symbolic graph optimization techniques, aiming for more efficient and effective high-level graph fusion and rewriting than either approach alone.

ideas.title: Inverse: A decompiler from traces to SGLang via Seismological Tomography
ideas.description: This research adapts the core solution from geophysical seismic tomography to the problem of decompiling low-level LM execution traces back into high-level SGLang programs. In seismology, the internal structure of the Earth is inferred by solving an inverse problem using surface measurements of seismic waves. Analogously, we propose to treat an SGLang program as the hidden structure and its execution trace (a sequence of low-level operations, intermediate states, and token emissions) as the observable output. The adapted solution is to build a tool that implements a probabilistic inversion model. This model will take a trace as input and perform a guided search through the combinatorial space of possible SGLang constructs—such as control flow primitives, parallel branches, and prefilling strategies—to reconstruct the most probable, concise, and idiomatic SGLang source program that would have generated the given low-level trace, effectively acting as a decompiler for LM execution.

ideas.title: Heterogeneous Agent Architectures with Compilation-Aware Learning
ideas.description: This research explores a multi-agent learning framework where agents possess fundamentally different learning algorithms and internal representations, intentionally designed to be compiled or translated into executable code with minimal latency. The compiler's primary objective shifts from generating maximally performant code for a single agent to rapidly synthesizing a cohesive, executable system from this heterogeneity. This allows the collective to dynamically reconfigure its computational substrate in response to new tasks or data, trading peak individual performance for agile systemic adaptation. Consequently, the system's intelligence emerges from the interplay between diverse learning strategies and the compiler's ability to quickly unify them into a functioning whole.

ideas.title: Dynamic KV Cache Management for Efficient Multimodal Model Evaluation
ideas.description: This research adapts the pairwise comparison framework to evaluate multimodal models (text, image, audio, video) by integrating a dynamic KV cache management system. The key innovation is to instrument the evaluation process to monitor and strategically compress or discard KV cache entries based on their cross-modal attention patterns and reuse value during inference on evaluation tasks. This allows the framework to not only rank model performance but also produce fine-grained diagnostic metrics about computational waste and inter-modal dependency, directly linking evaluation outcomes to specific architectural inefficiencies. Consequently, the approach provides a tool for guiding the design of more efficient multimodal architectures by pinpointing where cache reuse is minimal or detrimental across different input types.

ideas.title: Detecting and Quantifying Contamination in Curated Datasets Using Forensic Genomics Techniques
ideas.description: This research investigates the prevalence and impact of contamination in smaller, high-quality datasets by drawing an analogy from forensic genomics and ancient DNA research. In that field, contamination from modern DNA in precious, limited samples is addressed by creating a reference panel of potential contaminants and using alignment tools to detect and quantify them. Adapted to machine learning data, the idea is to build a library of digital 'contaminant fingerprints' from known, pervasive data sources. The study would then develop and apply forensic-inspired detection algorithms to scan curated datasets, measure contamination levels, and evaluate how this hidden noise affects model training and evaluation, ultimately proposing standards for contamination auditing in high-stakes data curation.

ideas.title: Provenance-Guided Speculative Optimization with Cost-Bounded Rollback for Dynamic Programs
ideas.description: This system enables aggressive optimization for programs with unknown or evolving structure by speculatively specializing code based on probabilistic models of graph evolution. It executes these specializations optimistically using hardware support for speculation and transactional memory. A continuous provenance tracker validates the speculative assumptions against the actual runtime evolution; violations trigger a fast, localized rollback and the installation of a corrected optimization. A global cost-benefit controller strictly regulates the optimization process, ensuring the overhead of monitoring and rollback never exceeds the performance gains, guaranteeing net speedup.

ideas.title: Adapting Drug Discovery's Active Learning for RL-Guided Program Transformation Search
ideas.description: This idea applies analogical reasoning from computational drug discovery, where reinforcement learning guides the sequential modification of molecules to optimize properties. The analogous solution uses RL to learn a policy for selecting promising molecular transformations within a vast combinatorial space. Adapted to program transformation, we propose an RL agent that learns a search policy for compiler optimizations. The agent treats the code's intermediate representation as its state, available transformations as actions, and the resulting program's performance as reward, thereby learning to construct high-performance transformation sequences adaptively, much like designing a drug candidate pathway.

ideas.title: Adapting Psychophysical Scaling for Multimodal Model Evaluation
ideas.description: The original idea to extend a pairwise comparison framework for multimodal models finds a strong analogy in psychophysics, the scientific study of the relationship between physical stimuli and subjective perception. In that field, complex problems like quantifying perceived sound quality or image fidelity are solved using standardized psychophysical scaling methods, such as paired comparisons and magnitude estimation, coupled with statistical psychometric models to create objective scales from subjective judgments. Adapting this solution, we propose a research initiative to develop a rigorous evaluation platform for multimodal AI. Human evaluators would be presented with pairs of outputs (e.g., two text descriptions of an image, two audio clips generated from a video) from different models and asked for their preference. This preference data would be aggregated and analyzed using models like Thurstone's or Bradley-Terry to rank the models on a continuous, interpretable scale of output quality, effectively creating a 'perceptual performance metric' that works seamlessly across text, image, audio, and video modalities.

ideas.title: Apply Forensic DNA Mixture Analysis to Detect Benchmark Contamination in Synthetic Data
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from forensic genetics to the problem of auditing synthetic datasets for hidden benchmark contamination. The analogous field is Forensic Genetics, where the analogous solution is the use of probabilistic genotyping to deconvolute mixed DNA samples into individual contributor profiles. This is adapted by treating a synthetic dataset generated by a model like GPT-4 as a forensic 'mixture.' Statistical and embedding-based models, analogous to probabilistic genotyping software, would be developed to deconvolute this data. The process would separate the 'signal' of novel, model-generated content from the 'signal' of memorized or subtly reproduced content from known benchmark datasets (the contaminants). The output would be a forensic-style report quantifying the proportion of contamination and identifying the likely source benchmark materials present within the synthetic data, providing a systematic audit trail.

ideas.title: Incorporate adversarial data collection techniques
ideas.description: Generate adversarial examples to systematically probe and strengthen LLM judges' failure modes.
ideas.sourceTitle: Judging LLM-as-a-judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena

ideas.title: Adaptive KV Cache Pruning via Signal Processing Thresholding
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core principle of lossy compression from signal processing to the KV cache problem. In fields like audio and image processing, transforms (e.g., FFT) identify which frequency components are perceptually significant; components below a dynamic threshold are discarded to achieve high compression with minimal quality loss. Similarly, we can research methods to project the KV cache into a latent space where each entry's contribution to future attention computations can be quantified as 'attention energy' or 'influence.' An adaptive thresholding mechanism, potentially based on the entropy or variance of the current context, would then efficiently discard or highly compress entries deemed minimally influential. This inverts the standard reuse paradigm by making aggressive, intelligent pruning the default, preserving reuse only where it is provably beneficial, thereby reducing memory footprint and potentially mitigating negative interference from stale cache entries.

ideas.title: Use memory compression from OS and graphics
ideas.description: Apply transparent memory compression techniques to KV cache pages under memory pressure.
ideas.sourceTitle: Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention

ideas.title: Multi-Modal Program Synthesis via Learned Query Optimization and Materialized View Planning
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel execution engine for complex multi-modal generation pipelines (e.g., text-to-image-to-video with critique and editing) by treating them as declarative programs to be optimized. It applies and extends core database techniques—such as cost-based query optimization, materialized view selection, and predicate pushdown—to the domain of neural program graphs. The key innovation is a learned optimizer that profiles the latency, cost, and output quality distributions of stochastic generative modules to build a cost model, enabling it to automatically rewrite pipeline DAGs, cache intermediate 'neural materialized views,' and parallelize branches in a resource-aware manner. This results in systems that can synthesize efficient execution plans for multi-stage generative tasks, significantly improving throughput and latency while preserving output fidelity.

ideas.title: Compiler-Optimized, Differentially Private Training for Contamination-Resistant Benchmark Generation
ideas.description: This research direction develops a compiler stack, extending concepts from TVM, that is specifically designed to optimize and compile entire differentially private training loops. The compiler's goal is to aggressively optimize the performance of the privacy-preserving operations (e.g., gradient clipping, noise addition) and the subsequent model updates, which are typically a bottleneck. This efficiency enables the practical use of strong differential privacy guarantees not just on final data, but within the training process itself to generate synthetic benchmark datasets. The outcome is an automated pipeline that can take a seed corpus and produce a high-utility, privacy-protected benchmark with provable resistance to contamination detection, as the final data is a synthesized product of a private training run rather than a noised version of the original inputs.

ideas.title: Assume hardware constraints are not static
ideas.description: Create an optimizer for hardware with dynamic frequency, thermal, or power constraints.
ideas.sourceTitle: Ansor : Generating High-Performance Tensor Programs for Deep Learning

ideas.title: On-Device Multimodal Decontamination via Micro-VTA
ideas.description: This research fuses scale decontamination with ultra-edge hardware to create a system where micro-VTA cores embedded in sensors (e.g., cameras, microphones) perform lightweight, multimodal semantic decontamination in real-time on the raw data stream. The synergy creates a new paradigm of 'pre-emptive decontamination,' where data is scrubbed of unwanted semantic content (e.g., faces, proprietary code snippets, sensitive audio) or distributional artifacts before it leaves the device. This drastically reduces bandwidth, enhances privacy, and ensures that only clean, task-relevant data is used for further on-device learning or selective upload, making large-scale multimodal dataset curation more efficient and secure from the source.

ideas.title: Template-Free Program Synthesis via Developmental Biology Principles
ideas.description: This idea inverts the reliance on a predefined schedule template by drawing an analogy from Evolutionary Developmental Biology (Evo-Devo). In that field, complex organismal forms emerge not from a fixed blueprint but from the self-organizing execution of simple, local developmental rules (like reaction-diffusion systems) under evolutionary guidance. The analogous solution is to specify generative principles rather than a final form. Adapted to program variant generation, the research proposes to replace the static schedule template with a minimal set of template-free generative principles—such as resource constraints, data-flow dependencies, and latency/throughput objectives. A search-based or evolutionary algorithm would then use these principles in a 'developmental' process, allowing the specific schedule structure and instruction variants to self-organize and emerge through iterative application and competition, directly shaped by the target hardware's performance feedback.

ideas.title: Profile-Guided Adaptive Kernel Fusion for Dynamic Language Model Graphs
ideas.description: This system overcomes the static limitations of traditional deep learning kernel fusion by making it adaptive to the runtime behavior of language models. It integrates a lightweight profiler that executes sample batches to capture the dynamic execution graph, data dependencies, and memory bottlenecks. A compiler then uses this profile to perform a constrained combinatorial search over potential fusion regions, generating custom CUDA kernels that are specifically optimized for the observed workload patterns. The result is a robust fusion strategy that automatically adapts to different model architectures, input sequence lengths, and batch sizes, ensuring performance gains where static fusion would fail.

ideas.title: Contamination as a Calibrated Stressor: Borrowing from Vaccine Development
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that data contamination is purely harmful by drawing an analogy to immunology. In vaccine development, controlled exposure to a pathogen is beneficial for building immunity. Similarly, we propose a method where machine learning models are exposed to controlled, structured 'contaminants'—such as calibrated noise, adversarial perturbations, or samples from known out-of-distribution datasets—during training. This deliberate contamination acts as a calibrated stressor, not to degrade performance on clean data, but to improve the model's calibration (the alignment between its predicted confidence and its actual accuracy) and to provide a measurable signal of its generalization capability. The solution adapts the immunological principle by designing contamination schedules and doses that strengthen the model's 'immune system' against real-world data impurities and distribution shifts.

ideas.title: Efficient management of non-contiguous block sequences
ideas.description: Improve handling when shared KV cache blocks are not in contiguous virtual address space.
ideas.sourceTitle: Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention

ideas.title: Decoupling KV cache layout from model architecture
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that KV cache structure is fixed per layer and head.
ideas.sourceTitle: Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention

ideas.title: Reactive Stream Processing for Dynamic Agent Orchestration
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a novel agent runtime architecture that treats both the agent's internal state (beliefs, tool outputs) and its control flow decisions as continuous, incrementally updatable data streams. By applying stream processing semantics—like windows, joins, and stateful operators—to the control plane itself, the system can efficiently manage highly dynamic workflows where the next step is determined by evolving data. This allows for speculative branching, proactive resource allocation, and the seamless merging of parallel execution paths, creating agents that are both reactive to real-time inputs and provably efficient in their orchestration logic.

ideas.title: Federated Prompt Execution for Massive Edge Inference
ideas.description: This research direction develops a system that compiles a single, complex prompt into an optimized execution plan that can be distributed across millions of edge and mobile devices. Each device runs a lightweight, specialized version of the runtime to process its local data or assigned shard of a global dataset with the shared prompt logic. The central orchestrator manages job distribution, handles device heterogeneity and churn, and aggregates results, transforming a fragmented edge landscape into a unified, massive-scale batch processing fabric. This fusion enables new applications like global sensor data analysis, personalized federated learning prompts, and privacy-aware content filtering at a previously impossible scale.

ideas.title: Stream-Aware Intermediate Representation for Incremental Agent Optimization
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel compiler architecture for language model agents that fuses a compiler intermediate representation (IR) with stream processing semantics. The agent's program is lifted into a stream-aware IR, modeling its components (e.g., tools, reasoning steps, memory) as operators in a continuous dataflow graph. This allows the application of traditional compiler optimization passes—like dead code elimination, constant propagation, and loop fusion—but in a dynamic, incremental manner as new data events and agent state updates flow through the graph. The result is a system that can automatically restructure and optimize a live agent's execution loop for latency, cost, and correctness without requiring a full recompilation or halting its operation.

ideas.title: Detect and Mitigate Hardware-Induced Semantic Drift via Inverse Primitive Synthesis
ideas.description: This research adapts plagiarism detection techniques to identify 'semantic contamination' or drift in model outputs caused by the use of specific hardware acceleration primitives, such as novel tensor cores. The inverse design component then uses these detected divergence patterns as a feedback signal. By analyzing the workloads and the specific semantic errors, the system automatically proposes new or modified hardware primitives optimized to preserve the original model's semantic integrity. This creates a co-design loop where hardware is iteratively refined to guarantee computational faithfulness, not just raw performance.

ideas.title: Multi-Tenant, Hardware-Aware Cache Orchestration for Shared GPU Clusters
ideas.description: This research develops a cache management and eviction framework for systems like H2O, explicitly designed for shared GPU clusters where multiple jobs (tenants) contend for memory resources. It moves beyond generic hardware tuning by creating performance models that fuse knowledge of specific GPU memory hierarchies (e.g., NVIDIA A100 vs. H100 bandwidths, cache sizes) with real-time telemetry on multi-tenant access patterns and interference. The core innovation is an orchestration layer that dynamically partitions and manages the cache structure, predicting and mitigating inter-tenant eviction storms and bandwidth contention, thereby guaranteeing quality-of-service and improving aggregate throughput across diverse, co-located workloads on shared hardware.

ideas.title: Apply anomaly detection to find novel failures
ideas.description: Use unsupervised anomaly detection on conversation sequences to find rare LLM failure modes.
ideas.sourceTitle: LMSYS-Chat-1M: A Large-Scale Real-World LLM Conversation Dataset

ideas.title: Adapting Metabolic Minimalism for Micro-Controller Optimization
ideas.description: This research adapts the concept of a minimal genome from systems biology to the problem of scaling down AI frameworks for micro-controller targets. In biology, defining a minimal genome involves identifying the essential genetic pathways for survival in a constrained environment. Analogously, we propose to define a 'minimal computational genome' for a given task. The framework will employ iterative pruning, compression, and pathway analysis to strip a model down to its core, essential operations required to meet target performance metrics, eliminating all redundant or non-critical computations. This creates a fundamentally minimal and efficient model architecture tailored to the extreme resource constraints of edge micro-controllers, optimizing for size, power, and latency from first principles.

ideas.title: Meta-Reinforcement Learning for Transferable Program Transformation Search
ideas.description: This system employs meta-reinforcement learning to acquire a transferable prior for selecting program transformations. It is pre-trained offline on a diverse dataset of code optimization and synthesis tasks, learning a latent space that captures the semantics and effects of transformations. This enables rapid, sample-efficient adaptation to new search spaces or objectives with minimal online trials. The approach overcomes the generalization and tuning bottlenecks of standard RL, yielding a robust and reusable policy for automated program improvement.

ideas.title: Optimization for multi-query and grouped-query attention
ideas.description: Tailor KV cache paging and sharing for models using multi-query or grouped-query attention variants.
ideas.sourceTitle: Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention

ideas.title: DualFlow: Learning to Prune Inefficient Paths in Dynamic Control Flow
ideas.description: This research direction trains a model to predict which branches, loops, or dataflow decisions in a program with highly dynamic control flow are likely to lead to inefficient execution paths. Instead of pruning a search space of static code variants, the system prunes the space of possible runtime execution trajectories. This enables aggressive optimization and speculative execution for dynamic programs by focusing computational resources on the predicted high-efficiency control flow paths, while quickly abandoning paths predicted to be suboptimal. The core novelty is using learned performance prediction to tame the complexity of data-dependent control flow, making it optimizable in a way neither static pruning nor dynamic execution alone can achieve.

ideas.title: Latent-Space Bayesian Optimization for Program Variants via Structured Embeddings
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the mismatch between BO's assumptions and the program variant space by first learning a continuous, smooth representation of that space. A graph neural network is trained on a corpus of program variants and their performance metrics to produce embeddings where program similarity and performance gradients are meaningful. Bayesian optimization then operates efficiently within this latent embedding space, proposing new points which are decoded back into concrete program variants for evaluation. This hybrid system combines the sample efficiency of BO with the representational power of deep learning for structured data, enabling effective search over complex, discrete program transformations.

ideas.title: Proactive Contamination Firewall for Synthetic Data Generation
ideas.description: This system embeds contamination prevention directly into the process of generating synthetic data with large language models. It operates in real-time, analyzing the model's internal states and output semantics during generation to identify and block data that resembles known benchmarks or exhibits anomalous memorization signatures. The flagged outputs are diverted to a quarantine for human review, ensuring only 'clean' data passes through. This transforms contamination from a post-hoc audit problem into a controlled, in-line manufacturing defect rate, enabling the scalable production of higher-integrity synthetic datasets.

ideas.title: Adaptive Runtime Orchestration for Dynamic Multi-Tool LLM Programs
ideas.description: This research fuses enhanced external tool integration with support for dynamic program structures to create a system where SGLang's frontend becomes an adaptive orchestrator. It would develop mechanisms to monitor the state of both the evolving computational graph and the connected external environments (e.g., databases, simulators, user interfaces), using this feedback to dynamically select, sequence, and optimize tool calls. The novel capability is a runtime that can efficiently execute programs where the next step—and the optimal tool for it—is determined by the results of previous steps and the live state of external systems, moving beyond static compilation for a predefined graph.

ideas.title: Dual: Learn to Predict Suboptimal Implementations via a Virtual Metabolic Screen
ideas.description: This idea adapts the high-throughput virtual screening approach from metabolic engineering to the problem of pruning a search space of program implementations. In metabolic engineering, computational models predict the viability and yield of pathway variants to avoid costly lab experiments on suboptimal designs. Analogously, we propose training a model (the 'Dual') to quickly predict key performance metrics—like runtime, memory use, or energy efficiency—for candidate code variants generated during superoptimization or program synthesis. This model acts as a fast, approximate performance simulator, learning to identify 'toxic' (inefficient) implementations. By integrating this predictor into the search process, the system can aggressively prune suboptimal branches, dramatically scaling the search by focusing computational resources only on the most promising regions of the implementation space, much like a virtual metabolic screen guides biological design.

ideas.title: Latency-Aware Learned Graph Optimization for Single-Query Inference
ideas.description: This research direction fuses latency-centric attention optimization with learned, high-level graph rewriting to create a novel optimization stack for interactive LLM inference. It develops a cost model that learns to predict the end-to-end latency impact of different graph-level transformations—such as operator fusion, kernel selection, and memory layout changes—under the specific constraints of a single forward pass. The system then uses this model to dynamically rewrite the computational graph, applying techniques like a latency-optimized variant of RadixAttention, to minimize total response time rather than aggregate throughput. This enables holistic optimizations that span from the graph structure down to the memory access patterns of individual kernels, tailored for the real-time demands of conversational AI.

ideas.title: Study the dual: LLM queries about humans
ideas.description: Analyze conversations where users ask LLMs to analyze or simulate human behavior.
ideas.sourceTitle: LMSYS-Chat-1M: A Large-Scale Real-World LLM Conversation Dataset

ideas.title: Adaptive Compilation via Ecological Succession Models
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of static program structure by drawing an analogy from ecology, specifically the study of ecological succession. In ecology, communities are not static; they evolve through predictable stages after a disturbance. This analogous problem is solved using predictive models (e.g., Markov chains) to forecast structural changes, enabling adaptive resource management. Adapted to compiler research, this suggests creating optimizations where the compiler, aided by profiling or lightweight runtime monitoring, builds a model predicting how the program's internal graph structure (call graph, dependency network) will transition between phases. The compiler can then generate code that is not optimized for a single, assumed structure, but which efficiently adapts its optimization strategy (e.g., reordering, recompiling hot paths, adjusting speculation guards) in anticipation of these modeled evolutionary steps, leading to better performance for programs with unknown or evolving runtime behavior.

ideas.title: Inverse: maximize accuracy per memory byte
ideas.description: Given a fixed high throughput, maximize model accuracy by optimizing precision allocation across layers.
ideas.sourceTitle: High-throughput Generative Inference of Large Language Models with a Single GPU

ideas.title: Relax assumption of single-turn evaluation sufficiency
ideas.description: Assume multi-turn conversational context is essential for accurate safety and quality assessment.
ideas.sourceTitle: LMSYS-Chat-1M: A Large-Scale Real-World LLM Conversation Dataset

ideas.title: Adapting Phylogenetic Contamination Detection for Semantic Analysis
ideas.description: This idea applies the core methodology from computational biology's phylogenetic contamination detection to the problem of semantic contamination. In genomics, algorithms identify horizontal gene transfer by finding genetic sequences that are anomalously similar to distant branches of the evolutionary tree, suggesting foreign incorporation. The adapted solution involves modeling the expected 'evolution' of ideas or narratives within a corpus (e.g., a research field, news cycle, or dataset) to establish a normative semantic phylogeny. Plagiarism-like text similarity algorithms would then be used not just for direct copying, but to detect semantic 'sequences' whose similarity profile places them as outliers in this phylogeny, thereby identifying ideational contamination from external, potentially misleading or conflating sources.

ideas.title: One-Time Cryptographic Benchmark Generation via Secure Multi-Party Computation
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from cryptography to solve the benchmark contamination problem. The analogous field is Cryptography and Secure Communications, specifically the use of one-time pads and Secure Multi-Party Computation (MPC) for generating and distributing secrets. The analogous solution involves multiple parties using MPC to collaboratively create a secret (like a one-time encryption key) that no single party knows in advance, ensuring its security until use. Adapted to AI evaluation, a consortium of research organizations would use an MPC protocol to jointly generate a novel, secure benchmark. Each party contributes private data or criteria, and the protocol outputs a complete, encrypted benchmark only at the moment of the official evaluation. This benchmark is used exactly once for model testing, after which the generation seeds and intermediate data are provably deleted. This ensures the benchmark is fresh, uncontaminated, and its contents cannot be reverse-engineered or leaked prior to the exam, providing a robust and trustworthy evaluation framework.

ideas.title: Integrate Ansor with more compiler frontends

ideas.title: Neurosymbolic Optimization of Probabilistic Program Graphs for LM-Generated Code
ideas.description: This system defines a Probabilistic Program Graph (PPG) as a unified IR for LM outputs, capturing not just syntactic structure but also uncertainty, alternative interpretations, and soft constraints. A neurosymbolic optimizer, combining traditional compiler passes with a learned transformation policy, reasons over the PPG to refine code, improve correctness, and align with user intent. This allows for the application of compiler techniques to the inherently non-deterministic and often underspecified programs produced by LMs, bridging the gap between formal analysis and generative AI.

ideas.title: Apply mean-field game theory
ideas.description: Use mean-field approximations to analyze equilibria in massive agent populations.

ideas.title: Controlled Contamination as a Diagnostic for Memorization and Generalization Boundaries
ideas.description: This research proposes a rigorous framework where controlled, traceable data contamination is used not as a training benefit but as a diagnostic tool. By systematically injecting identifiable 'probe' examples into training and meticulously tracking model performance on clean data, the contaminant probes themselves, and related out-of-distribution tasks, the method quantifies the trade-off between memorization and generalization. The core output is a set of metrics and visualizations that delineate a model's generalization boundary, showing how much exposure to a data family leads to overfitting versus robust learning. This turns contamination from an uncontrolled variable into a measurable axis for auditing model reliability and calibration under data pollution scenarios.

ideas.title: Adapting Immunological Memory for LLM Decontamination
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from immunology/vaccinology to strengthen LLM-based semantic decontamination. In vaccinology, introducing a weakened pathogen trains the immune system for future, stronger responses. Adapted to this research context, the method involves proactively training the language model on carefully constructed 'attenuated' or semantically transformed versions of known benchmark and test data (e.g., paraphrased, ablated, or perturbed examples). This process builds the model's internal 'immune memory' against specific contamination patterns, making it more robust to accidental exposure during pre-training. The goal is to refine decontamination by moving beyond passive filtering to active immunization, thereby improving the model's ability to generalize from its training data without simply memorizing benchmark answers.

ideas.title: Secure Multi-Tenant Execution with Provable Side-Channel Resistance
ideas.description: This system provides secure performance isolation for multi-tenant shared hardware by explicitly defending against side-channel attacks, not just performance noise. It employs a lightweight hypervisor or runtime layer that dynamically allocates and partitions hardware resources (caches, memory banks, execution ports) using hardware features like Intel CAT or AMD SEV-SNP, guided by a proven information-flow security policy. The design continuously audits resource usage patterns to detect and mitigate potential covert channels, ensuring confidentiality guarantees are maintained even under adversarial co-location. This enables safe hardware sharing for sensitive workloads without sacrificing the efficiency gains of multi-tenancy.

ideas.title: Scale up to multi-GPU, low-memory nodes
ideas.description: Extend the aggregation approach to many small-memory GPUs instead of one GPU plus CPU/disk.
ideas.sourceTitle: High-throughput Generative Inference of Large Language Models with a Single GPU

ideas.title: Contrastive Anomaly Detection for Latent Semantic Contamination
ideas.description: This system proactively identifies semantic contamination by modeling a language model's expected reasoning patterns rather than searching for text matches. It trains a detector using contrastive learning on curated, verified uncontaminated data to establish a baseline 'reasoning fingerprint'. The detector then flags outputs that exhibit statistical anomalies in conceptual association strength, argument graph structure, or inference pathways, indicating influence from unseen semantic sources. This approach detects contamination from private, paraphrased, or conceptually transferred content that plagiarism techniques would miss.

ideas.title: Using Ecological Indicator Species to Validate Contaminated Benchmarks
ideas.description: This research investigates if performance on contaminated benchmarks still correlates with useful real-world skills by drawing an analogy from ecology. In ecology, scientists use 'indicator species' to gauge ecosystem health, validating them against tangible functional outcomes even in polluted environments. The adapted solution is to treat a set of novel, uncontaminated real-world tasks as 'indicator tasks' for true AI capability. The research would empirically test the correlation between scores on a widely used but potentially contaminated benchmark and performance on these independent indicator tasks, thereby determining if the benchmark remains a valid predictor of underlying ability or has become a measure of artifact exploitation.

ideas.title: Hardware-Aware Synthesis and Verification for Micro-Controller Deployment
ideas.description: This research designs a system that automatically restructures and verifies framework components for deployment on specific micro-controller targets. It moves beyond simple scaling by using a formal model of the target hardware's constraints to drive a synthesis process that generates custom, verifiably correct kernels and memory layouts. This ensures the resulting optimized code is not only functionally equivalent but also guaranteed to meet the extreme resource limits of the device. The outcome is a robust, automated pipeline from high-level models to deployable micro-controller code that is provably resource-safe.

ideas.title: Controlled Memorization Audit and Semantic Equivalence Evaluation
ideas.description: This research first defines and induces measurable memorization by training or fine-tuning models on a secret, marked subset of benchmark data. It then applies targeted unlearning techniques aimed at removing this specific memorization while preserving general task performance. The core evaluation measures the model's performance on the original benchmark questions versus novel, paraphrased questions that probe the same underlying knowledge, directly testing if the model relies on memorized artifacts or robust understanding. This provides a clear, quantifiable metric for the success of forgetting interventions.

ideas.title: Online Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Optimization of Non-Stationary LM Execution Graphs
ideas.description: This system addresses the non-stationarity of LM components by replacing static database-style optimization with an online learning framework. A meta-optimizer continuously probes the execution environment, gathering live telemetry on node performance to build a dynamic cost and reliability model. Using this model, it applies a reinforcement learning policy to decide on graph rewrites, parallelization, and fallback strategies in real-time. This results in a self-improving execution engine that adapts to changing model APIs, prompt sensitivities, and computational budgets, robustly optimizing for user-defined trade-offs between speed, cost, and accuracy.

ideas.title: Decontamination via Light-Token Analysis and Pruning (DeLTA-P)
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using the identification of 'light tokens'—tokens consistently unimportant for a model's core capabilities—as a novel signal for detecting and removing contaminated or low-quality data. By establishing that contaminated data often manifests as statistically anomalous patterns in these light token distributions, we can define protocols to prune or quantize these token pathways, thereby decontaminating the model's effective training set post-hoc. This enables a shift from pre-training dataset filtering to efficient, targeted model editing for safety and quality. The synergy yields a proactive decontamination standard that is both more scalable and more precise than manual dataset auditing alone.

ideas.title: Dual-Online: Self-Improving Predictor for Adaptive Search Space Pruning
ideas.description: This system trains a model to predict suboptimal program variants but operates within an online learning framework. Instead of static pruning, it uses the predictor to guide a dynamic search, while periodically validating its predictions with lightweight runtime measurements or cost-model checks. Prediction errors are automatically curated into a continuous training dataset, enabling the model to adapt to new target architectures, compiler updates, and previously unseen code patterns. This creates a robust, domain-adaptive system that safely prunes the search space without getting trapped by the limitations of its initial training data.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Impact of IR-Level Contamination on Cross-Platform Auto-Scheduler Generalization
ideas.description: This research fuses the concepts of contamination tolerance and multi-frontend auto-scheduling by investigating how the inevitable semantic and structural 'contamination' introduced when mapping diverse IRs (e.g., MLIR, TorchScript) into Ansor's search space impacts benchmark results and scheduler generalization. Instead of requiring perfect, lossless IR lowering, the work will empirically determine thresholds of contamination—such as operator fusion differences or layout annotation loss—beyond which auto-scheduler performance significantly degrades or becomes unfairly biased toward one source IR. The outcome is a contamination-aware extension to Ansor that can adapt its search strategy based on detected levels of IR impurity, promoting more robust and portable performance predictions across the heterogeneous compiler ecosystem.

ideas.title: Hybrid Synthesis-Guided Stochastic Superoptimization for Tensor Programs
ideas.description: This approach combines the correctness guarantees of formal synthesis with the scalability of machine learning-guided search. A synthesis engine generates a library of verified, correct-by-construction micro-kernels and transformation rules for tensor operations. A meta-optimizer then uses these rules as atomic actions within a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) framework, where a neural cost model prunes the action space and predicts performance. This ensures the exploration of variants is both efficient and anchored in semantically correct transformations, making the technique practical for integration into production compilers like TVM or MLIR.

ideas.title: Calibrating Automated Benchmarks via Human Preference Data as a Ground Truth Proxy
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution of item calibration from psychometrics to the problem of integrating automated evaluation metrics with human preference data. In educational testing, new questions are statistically calibrated against a validated baseline to ensure they measure the intended construct. Analogously, we propose to treat the human preference data from the Arena as a robust, albeit noisy, ground truth baseline. The adapted research idea is to develop a calibration framework that uses a large set of model outputs with paired human preference votes and automated metric scores. By applying statistical models (e.g., from Item Response Theory or similar correlation/regression techniques), we can estimate the reliability, discrimination, and potential bias of each automated metric relative to the human data. This would create a unified, interpretable scale for automated benchmarks, identifying which metrics are strong, direct proxies for human judgment and which require correction or contextual understanding, thereby enabling more trustworthy integration of automated evaluation into model development and ranking.

ideas.title: Proactive, Utility-Aware KV Cache Management via Continuous Salience Scoring
ideas.description: This research designs a transformer inference system where KV cache management is seamlessly integrated into the attention mechanism itself. Each key-value pair is tagged at generation time with a lightweight, learned salience score based on its attention distribution and token information. The cache operates as a fixed-size priority buffer, where incoming KV pairs automatically displace the lowest-scoring existing entries. This eliminates the need for separate, error-prone prediction modules, ensures high-utility context is retained by construction, and turns cache maintenance into a constant-time operation with no additional latency.

ideas.title: Tracer Injection for Data Provenance and Integrity Mapping
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from hydrology's tracer tests to the AI training context. The analogous problem is understanding how a contaminant moves through a complex, opaque system (groundwater vs. a neural network). The analogous solution is the controlled injection of a detectable tracer. The adapted research idea is to deliberately contaminate training data with engineered 'tracer' patterns—semantically plausible yet uniquely identifiable artifacts—to study their propagation and detection in models. This inverse approach allows for the empirical mapping of influence pathways within the model, the quantification of how contamination scales with model and dataset size (SCALING_DUAL), and the development of robust forensic techniques to audit training data provenance and detect unauthorized data use or poisoning.

ideas.title: Apply game theory to model strategic interactions
ideas.description: Frame user-LLM dialogue as a sequential game to model persuasion or deception.

ideas.title: Ab Initio Hardware Synthesis via Differentiable Physical Simulation
ideas.description: This system co-designs radically new hardware primitives and the programs they execute by escaping the bias of existing architectures. It uses a generative model, guided by fundamental physical constraints, to propose novel compute and memory structures. These proposals are evaluated not by historical cost models, but by a differentiable simulator that models electronic behavior from the device level upward, directly measuring the performance of benchmark kernels. The resulting feedback loop discovers efficient architectures for future computational patterns that have no efficient realization in today's hardware design space.

ideas.title: Fault-Tolerant Stream Processing for LM Agents with Checkpoint/Rollback and State Validation
ideas.description: This system adapts stream processing for LM agents while directly combating error propagation. It structures the agent's continuous loop as a series of discrete, immutable state updates, each paired with a validity assessment. The core innovation is a managed state layer that maintains versioned checkpoints, allowing the system to roll back from erroneous updates triggered by model hallucinations or flawed reasoning. This enables the benefits of incremental processing while ensuring long-horizon task reliability by containing and repairing local failures.

ideas.title: Invert specialization for general-purpose use
ideas.description: Adapt VTA's architecture to efficiently run non-DL, data-parallel workloads.

ideas.title: Apply Compiler Loop Optimization to LM Execution Graphs
ideas.description: This idea adapts loop optimization techniques from compiler design to the problem of optimizing complex Language Model program execution graphs. In compilers, loops are analyzed for invariant code, unrolled, or fused to minimize redundant operations and improve hardware utilization. Analogously, LM execution graphs for agentic workflows, chained reasoning, or iterative refinement often contain repetitive structures. By treating these as 'loops' within the execution graph, we can develop a graph optimizer that performs operations akin to loop-invariant code motion (hoisting expensive but constant LM prompts), loop fusion (combining sequential refinement steps), and loop unrolling (expanding fixed iterations) on the computational graph before execution. This reduces the total number of costly LM calls and intermediate steps, directly optimizing for latency and cost, much like a database query planner optimizes a query plan for estimated runtime.

ideas.title: Benchmarking Contamination's Impact on Real-World Skill Transfer
ideas.description: This research creates a standardized contamination suite where the degree and type of data exposure are systematically controlled and documented. It then uses this suite to run a dual-focus experiment: first, evaluating decontamination methods, and second, crucially, testing if models with known contamination levels perform differently on novel, real-world tasks that require the same underlying skills the benchmark purports to measure. This allows for a direct, empirical investigation into whether high scores on contaminated benchmarks stem from memorization or from learned, transferable competencies. The outcome is a calibrated understanding of which benchmarks remain valid indicators of ability even after contamination, and under what conditions contamination fatally undermines their predictive value.

ideas.title: Develop more sophisticated bias mitigation techniques

ideas.sourceTitle: Judging LLM-as-a-judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena

ideas.title: Adapting Immunological Tolerance to Prevent Benchmark Memorization
ideas.description: This idea inverts the focus from data to model memory by studying a model's ability to forget or ignore memorized benchmark data. It draws an analogy from immunology, specifically the biological process of immunological tolerance, where the immune system learns not to attack the body's own tissues. The analogous solution involves mechanisms like clonal deletion and anergy during development to prevent autoimmunity. Adapted to machine learning, the research proposes a 'tolerance training' paradigm. After initial pre-training or fine-tuning, the model undergoes a secondary phase where it is re-exposed to benchmark or evaluation data with a modified objective: to learn to treat these specific data points as non-predictive 'self' information. This could be implemented via adversarial objectives or loss functions that actively discourage memorization and high confidence on the benchmark set, aiming to induce a form of 'anergy' towards that data. The goal is to create models whose evaluation more accurately reflects generalization on novel, non-memorized tasks, effectively 'forgetting' the benchmark for a purer assessment of capability.

ideas.title: Dynamic Verifiable Evaluation Protocol (DVEP): A Statistically Robust Benchmarking Ecosystem
ideas.description: This system establishes a public, cryptographically secure protocol for the continuous generation of novel, high-quality evaluation tasks from a maintained and evolving specification. Instead of a single one-time exam, evaluators use the protocol to generate a large, stratified sample of tasks, creating a statistically reliable benchmark suite for each evaluation round. The protocol's design and all generation seeds are public and verifiable, ensuring transparency and preventing contamination, while the volume and diversity of generated tasks provide a robust measure of true model capability and generalization.

ideas.title: Planetary-Scale Simulation Decontamination via Emergent Semantic Filtering
ideas.description: This research integrates advanced LLM-based semantic decontamination directly into the runtime fabric of planet-scale agent simulations, such as global IoT networks or digital twin societies. Instead of merely cleaning training data, the LLM continuously monitors the simulation's emergent communication and behavior patterns, using its semantic understanding to detect and neutralize novel, system-scale contamination events as they arise. This creates simulations that are not only massive but also robust and trustworthy, capable of self-regulating against cascading failures or coordinated adversarial attacks that only become apparent at planetary scale. The core challenge is developing efficient, real-time inference methods that allow the LLM to operate as an active, scalable component of the simulation itself, filtering pathological semantics without crippling performance.

ideas.title: Causal Mediation Analysis to Disentangle Benchmark Memorization from Latent Skill Acquisition
ideas.description: This research moves beyond correlation to establish a causal link between benchmark performance and underlying abilities. It designs a causal graph where benchmark scores, latent skills, and model capacity are distinct nodes. Using a suite of contaminated benchmarks and novel, uncontaminated skill probes, the study performs targeted model interventions (e.g., activation patching, linear probing) to measure the causal flow of information. The core analysis tests whether the latent skill variable is a necessary mediator for benchmark success, thereby determining if high scores reflect genuine capability or are bypassed via memorization and surface patterns.
