ideas.title: Adaptive Stream-Optimized Execution Planner for Continuous LM Agent Programs
ideas.description: This research fuses database query optimization with stream processing semantics to create a novel execution engine for long-running, multi-step LM agent programs. The core innovation is a continuous query planner that treats the agent's tool-calling loops, reflection steps, and sub-agent spawns as a dynamic dataflow graph over infinite streams of observations and user intents. By applying cost-based optimization—using runtime statistics on tool latency, token generation speed, and stream window characteristics—the system can perform live plan rewrites. This allows it to adaptively switch between eager and lazy execution, re-prioritize sub-tasks based on changing data arrival rates, and cache or pre-fetch intermediate results across loop iterations, all while maintaining correctness guarantees for stateful, incremental computation.

ideas.title: Robust, Transfer-Aware Cost Modeling with Fallback Mechanisms
ideas.description: This research develops a system that prevents negative transfer when moving cost models between workloads. It introduces a transferability estimator that assesses architectural and computational similarity before any model deployment. Based on this estimate, the system selects from a portfolio of strategies: fine-tuning a transferred model, learning a small residual correction, or initiating sample-efficient online learning from scratch. This approach guarantees that cost predictions are never worse than a safe baseline, making the overall method robust to diverse and unseen operators and network architectures.

ideas.title: Symbolic Shape-Invariant Cost Modeling with Online Adaptation for Dynamic Tensors
ideas.description: This system introduces a symbolic cost modeling framework where performance characteristics are learned as functions of symbolic shape variables and their relational constraints (e.g., `M`, `N`, `K`), rather than concrete values. This allows the compiler to generate and compare kernel performance predictions for any valid shape instantiation at runtime. To guarantee robustness against modeling inaccuracies, the system incorporates a lightweight online adaptation loop, where a small subset of promising kernels is profiled with the actual runtime shape, and the symbolic models are updated in real-time to finalize the optimal kernel selection. This approach ensures near-optimal performance across the infinite space of dynamic shapes, even for previously unseen configurations.

ideas.title: Adaptive Just-in-Time Compilation for Multi-Modal Pipelines using a Computational DAG Scheduler
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to develop runtime optimizations for complex, multi-stage multi-modal generation pipelines by drawing an analogy from High-Performance Computing (HPC). In HPC, complex scientific simulations are optimized using Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) schedulers combined with Just-in-Time (JIT) compilation to fuse operations and generate hardware-specific kernels. We adapt this solution by designing a runtime system for multi-modal AI pipelines. The system would statically analyze the pipeline DAG, profile execution to identify critical paths and compatible adjacent stages, and then apply a JIT compiler to fuse these stages into single, optimized computational kernels. This approach aims to minimize framework overhead and data transfer latency, dynamically optimizing the pipeline's execution graph for the specific hardware (e.g., GPU, NPU) in use, thereby significantly improving throughput and reducing latency for end-to-end multi-modal generation tasks.

ideas.title: Differentially Private Task-Space Sampling for Contamination-Opaque Benchmark Generation
ideas.description: This system generates benchmarks that are fundamentally opaque to contamination detection by applying differential privacy at the level of compositional task design. Instead of merely privatizing individual data points, the approach defines a formal space of task variants that are solution-equivalent but syntactically and structurally diverse. A differentially private algorithm samples from this space to construct the benchmark, ensuring the final collection's defining characteristics—its task logic, solution patterns, and stylistic fingerprints—are not uniquely identifiable. This makes it statistically impossible for an adversary to determine if a model was trained on the benchmark or on a plausibly similar, non-private distribution of tasks.

ideas.title: Dynamic Shape-Aware Compilation as a Library for Existing ML Frameworks
ideas.description: This research develops a library that integrates directly into frameworks like PyTorch and JAX, adding a just-in-time compilation layer specifically designed for dynamic computational graphs. It extends existing auto-scheduling and cost models to reason about and optimize for shape-invariant patterns and symbolic shape constraints, rather than fixed dimensions. The system profiles runtime shape distributions to generate and cache multiple optimized kernel variants, dynamically selecting the best one during execution. This allows developers to retain their familiar programming interface while achieving near-optimal hardware performance for models with data-dependent control flow, variable-length sequences, or dynamic neural architectures.

ideas.title: Adaptive Prompt Portfolio System for Robust Massive-Batch Inference
ideas.description: This system addresses the single-point-of-failure risk of a monolithic prompt by automating the creation and empirical testing of multiple prompt variants before full-scale deployment. It runs a small but representative validation batch through a candidate portfolio, using predefined success metrics to select the most effective and reliable prompt strategy for the full job. During the massive batch execution, it continuously samples outputs to detect performance degradation, enabling automatic fallback to a secondary prompt or halting for human review to prevent large-scale systematic error.

ideas.title: Concept-Anchored, Cross-Modal Pairwise Evaluation for Multimodal Models
ideas.description: This research develops a robust framework for evaluating multimodal AI models by solving the core challenge of cross-modal alignment in comparisons. Instead of simply adapting text-based pairwise methods, it introduces 'concept-anchoring,' where a specific, shared concept is defined and elicited through a unified prompt. Human evaluators then judge which of two model outputs—which could be in entirely different modalities like audio, image, or text—more faithfully and effectively instantiates that anchored concept. This approach ensures comparisons are meaningful and consistent, providing a principled way to benchmark multimodal understanding and generation capabilities beyond single-modality performance.

ideas.title: Synthesizing Cache-Aware Adapter Programs via Formal Program Synthesis
ideas.description: This research fuses program synthesis with database caching algorithms to create a system that automatically generates correct-by-construction tensor programs for adapters, where the synthesis is explicitly guided by formal models of cache policies (e.g., LRU, LFU). Instead of managing pre-written adapter code with a cache, the system synthesizes novel, functionally equivalent adapter program variants that are optimized for the specific eviction and prefetching behavior of the target caching strategy. This results in adapter implementations that minimize cache misses and memory footprint by construction, fundamentally co-designing the algorithm and its runtime memory management. The synergy creates a new capability: generating hardware-and-policy-aware adapter code that is impossible to derive through manual optimization or static caching alone.

ideas.title: Cost-Based Optimization for Contamination-Aware Machine Learning Pipelines
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel framework that integrates the principles of database query optimization with the study of data contamination in machine learning. The system models the training pipeline as a series of relational algebra-like operations on data tensors, where different paths (e.g., data cleaning methods, model architectures, validation checks) have associated computational costs and 'contamination risk' costs derived from intentional contamination studies. A cost-based optimizer dynamically generates an execution plan that minimizes a combined objective function of runtime, resource usage, and expected degradation or detectability of contaminants, enabling efficient, robustness-aware pipeline construction.

ideas.title: Polyhedral Contamination Analysis: Formal Detection of Data Leakage in ML Pipeline Kernels
ideas.description: This research applies polyhedral model techniques from high-performance computing to formally analyze the dataflow graphs and loop nests within machine learning pipelines that process small, curated datasets. The core idea is to model potential contamination—such as the inadvertent inclusion of validation data features during training—as specific types of illegal affine dependencies or data reuse patterns across pipeline stages. By representing the dataset indexing, augmentation, splitting, and batching operations in a polyhedral framework, the method can automatically prove the absence of certain contamination classes or identify the exact computational steps and data indices where leakage occurs, providing rigorous, explainable guarantees for critical datasets in fields like biomedicine or law.

ideas.title: Apply Bayesian Optimization for Program Search, Inspired by Drug Discovery
ideas.description: This idea adapts the successful application of Bayesian optimization from computational drug discovery to the problem of navigating the vast space of program variants. In drug discovery, Bayesian optimization guides the search for optimal molecules by modeling the expensive 'experiment' (lab synthesis and testing) with a cheap surrogate. Analogously, we can treat the compilation and execution of a program variant as an expensive experiment. We will use Bayesian optimization to build a probabilistic model (e.g., a Gaussian Process) that predicts a program variant's performance based on its features (e.g., configuration parameters). This model will then guide the search, proposing the most promising variant to evaluate next, thereby finding high-performance variants with far fewer compilations and benchmark runs than brute-force or random search.

ideas.title: Distributed SGLang via Computational Ecology's Metacommunity Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea challenges the single, centralized runtime assumption in SGLang by drawing an analogy from computational ecology's metacommunity dynamics. In ecology, metacommunities consist of multiple, heterogeneous patches (local communities) that maintain overall system function through migration and asynchronous interaction, without a central controller. The analogous solution involves modeling resource distribution and resilience across spatially separated units. Adapted to SGLang, the research would design a distributed runtime where each heterogeneous device or cluster operates as an independent 'patch' with a specialized function (e.g., prompt parsing, KV cache management, speculative decoding). A bio-inspired 'dispersal' protocol would manage the migration of execution states (like tokens or partial attention results) between patches based on real-time load and capability, enabling efficient execution across clusters without a monolithic central runtime. This approach inherently accommodates hardware heterogeneity and dynamic network conditions.

ideas.title: SGLang Semantic Backplane: Deep, Runtime-Native Integration for Tools and Environments
ideas.description: This research designs a deep integration layer, the Semantic Backplane, which moves beyond API wrappers to formally model external tools and interactive environments as first-class primitives within the SGLang runtime. It defines a common intermediate representation for tool-augmented computation graphs, allowing the runtime to perform optimizations like speculative execution, parallel tool calls, and semantic caching. This enables the construction of complex, stateful agentic workflows with significantly lower latency and higher robustness than chained API calls, while presenting a coherent programming model to the developer.

ideas.title: Bayesian Active Preference Optimization for Program Synthesis
ideas.description: This research direction integrates Bayesian optimization and active learning into a unified framework for program synthesis. A Bayesian model maintains a belief over both the performance landscape of program variants and the latent human preference function. An active learning module, using an acquisition function like expected improvement or uncertainty sampling, selects pairs of program outputs for human comparison that are most informative for refining this preference model. The system then uses the updated preference model to guide the Bayesian optimizer's search for new, high-quality program variants, creating a feedback loop that efficiently converges on programs optimized for human judgment.

ideas.title: Use Differential Privacy with Adaptive Noise Injection for Benchmark Creation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from epidemiology—where public health data releases use adaptive noise injection (like data swapping) based on local population sensitivity to protect privacy—to the problem of creating ML benchmarks resistant to contamination detection. The analogous epidemiological problem is releasing useful aggregate disease statistics without allowing re-identification of individuals, especially in small, unique populations. The adapted research idea employs a differential privacy framework for benchmark creation that goes beyond a fixed privacy parameter (epsilon). It dynamically adjusts the level of noise or transformation applied to each potential benchmark example based on an analysis of its 'uniqueness' or 'identifiability' within the candidate data pool. This creates a benchmark where the most easily detectable 'contamination fingerprints' are strategically obfuscated, making it significantly harder for models to identify and overfit to benchmark data, while preserving the benchmark's utility for evaluating model generalization on common patterns and tasks.
